Research Article - Peer Reviewed Conference Proceeding ESCAPE 35 - European Symposium on Computer Aided Process Engineering Ghent, Belgium. 6-9 July 2025 Jan F.M. Van Impe, Grégoire Léonard, Satyajeet S. Bhonsale, Monika E. Polańska, Filip Logist (Eds.) https://doi.org/10.69997/sct.174465 Syst Control Trans 4:1682-1687 (2025) 1682 Hybrid machine-learning for dynamic plant-wide biomanufacturing Shabnam Shahhoseynia*, Arijit Chakrabortyb, Mohammad Reza Boskabadia, Venkat Venkatasubramanianb, Seyed Soheil Mansouria a Department of Chemical and Biochemical Engineering, Technical University of Denmark, DK-2800 Kgs Lyngby, Denmark b Department of Chemical Engineering, Columbia University, New York, NY 10027, United States of America * Corresponding Author: shabsh@kt.dtu.dk

## ABSTRACT

This study focuses on biomanufacturing case study, i.e. Lovastatin production, employing a hybrid modeling framework that combines mechanistic and data-driven approaches. A time-series dataset was generated using the KT-Biologics I (KTB1) plantwide model, a dynamic simulation of continuous biomanufacturing. The dataset captures critical parameters such as nutrient concentrations and API production. The AI-DARWIN framework was used to develop interpretable machine learning models with constrained functional forms, ensuring both accuracy and clarity. The resulting polynomial-based models reveal key relationships between process variables and system performance, bridging mechanistic insights with data-driven predictions. The models demonstrated reasonable accuracy showing minimal difference between the training and testing errors, highlighting their strong generalization. This work advances hybrid modeling in biomanufacturing by integrating plant-wide mechanistic simulations with interpretable machine learning. The approach ensures both accuracy and transparency while enabling robust process monitoring and control at a ‘plant-wide’ level, contributing to the broader adoption of hybrid modeling in biomanufacturing. Keywords: Hybrid modeling, Biomanufacturing, Plant-wide modeling, Lovastatin production, Interpretable machine learning.

## 1. INTRODUCTION

The biomanufacturing of high-value products, such as Lovastatin, presents unique challenges due to the complex interplay of biological, chemical, and physical processes [1]. Lovastatin, a widely used cholesterol-lowering drug, is produced through microbial fermentation, where variations in nutrient concentrations, reactor conditions, and downstream processing steps significantly influence its yield and quality. Developing accurate and interpretable models to predict and optimize production outcomes is essential for improving efficiency and meeting stringent quality standards. Traditional modeling approaches in biomanufacturing often rely on mechanistic models, which are grounded in physical and biochemical laws. Developing these mechanistic models can sometimes be challenging, primarily due to the lack of advanced sensing technologies to gather data (for parameter estimation, model validation, etc.) and a limited understanding of multiscale phenomenological dynamics. However, datadriven modeling has shown great promise in capturing the behavior of complex systems, if and only, there is sufficient amount of data and right measurement of key characteristic phenomena in a process [2]. So, incorporating domain expertise into the modeling framework is crucial. Bioprocesses, as a prime example of complex systems, are ideal candidates for data-driven modeling, which help bridge gaps in both theoretical knowledge and practical modeling [3]. Although this approach can handle complex, nonlinear relationships in data, It often lacks transparency, making it difficult to trust and interpret its predictions in regulatory and operational contexts. To address this limitation, hybrid modeling approaches that combine mechanistic models with data- Shahhoseyni et al. / LAPSE:2025.0422 Syst Control Trans 4:1682-1687 (2025) 1683 driven techniques have gained significant attention. By combining numeric AI (machine learning) with symbolic AI, a hybrid AI approach can be developed, resulting in robust, interpretable models suitable for complex systems [4]. KT-Biologics I (KTB1) is a dynamic mechanistic simulation model for continuous biomanufacturing, covering the entire Lovastatin production plant [1]. While effective in capturing process mechanisms, KTB1 has long running times and does not fully clarify how process variables influence the final output, limiting its use for real-time monitoring and optimization. To address these limitations, a hybrid modeling approach integrates mechanistic insights with data-driven modeling. The AI-DARWIN framework [5] enables the discovery of interpretable polynomial models by constraining functional forms, ensuring both accuracy and clarity. This approach accelerates computation, enhances understanding of variable relationships, and improves process control efficiency. This work develops a hybrid model for Lovastatin biomanufacturing, aiming to create an interpretable representation of the process that captures its dynamic behavior, variable interactions, and plant-wide complexities. The approach begins by generating time-series datasets from the KTB1 model under varying nutrient conditions to systematically examine the effects of key variables, such as lactose and adenine, on Lovastatin production. These datasets feed into the AI-DARWIN framework, which builds explainable ML models, laying the foundation for future optimization of nutrient utilization and enabling knowledge transfer to new process scenarios, such as using different microorganisms or producing alternative products.

## 2. SYSTEM DESCRIPTION

### 2.1. KTB1 Model Description

This model presents a comprehensive framework encompassing both the upstream production and downstream purification of Lovastatin, a target compound produced via fermentation. As depicted in Figure 1, the upstream process begins with the introduction of key raw materials—adenine and lactose—into a mixing unit (M101). The mixed medium is then continuously fed into a Continuous Stirred-Tank Reactor (CSTR, labeled R-101), where the biomass, predominantly Aspergillus terreus, facilitates Lovastatin production. The fermentation broth exiting the CSTR is directed to a hydro-cyclone separator (HC-101), which plays a pivotal role in separating the mixture into two streams. The first stream, rich in biomass, is partially recycled back to the CSTR to sustain fermentation. The second stream, with lower biomass content (stream 5), is transferred to the downstream process. In the downstream phase, the focus is on a sequence of separation and purification steps. Centrifugation units (C-101 and C-102) initially remove remaining biomass and dense impurities from the solution. Following centrifugation, the process advances to nanofiltration (NF-101), where Lovastatin is concentrated by retaining larger molecules through steric exclusion while smaller impurities pass through. Figure 1 illustrates the complete process flow for the biomanufacturing of Lovastatin, including both the upstream and downstream operations.

### Figure 1. Process flow for the biomanufacturing of

Lovastatin 2.1.1 Dataset The datasets used in this study were generated to systematically explore the relationships between nutrient concentrations and Lovastatin API production. Two distinct datasets were created using the KTB1 mechanistic model, each designed to capture specific aspects of the nutrient dynamics in the biomanufacturing process. The datasets include concentrations of various nutrients (such as lactose and adenine), biomass levels in different streams, and the Lovastatin API produced production plant. In these datasets, only one nutrient concentration was varied at a time (by step change) while keeping all other process parameters constant. This method of isolating single variables ensures a controlled environment to study the individual effects of lactose and adenine on Lovastatin production. Each full dataset consists of about 20000 data points. We used 75% for training and 25% for testing, resulting in 15000 training samples and 5000 test samples. Dataset 1 contains information on altering lactose concentration (CLA). And Dataset 2 contains information on altering adenine concentration (CAD). The graphs corresponding to the generated datasets are presented in Figure 2. A step change in lactose at the start of the process, along with a noisy but constant adenine Shahhoseyni et al. / LAPSE:2025.0422 Syst Control Trans 4:1682-1687 (2025) 1684 input, were introduced to the model. The noise was intentionally added to simulate real-world scenarios, particularly in nutrient supply, where fluctuations are often observed in one of the inputs. Step changes in lactose and adenine, as seen in the first and second datasets, can arise from sensor malfunctions, control system errors, or other process deviations, which are common faults in industrial processes. These variations result in changes to other system variables, such as biomass concentration and flow rates. Figure 2 illustrates how these variations cause dynamic changes in some of the other variables (C,X,4 and Q,R).

### 2.2. AI-DARWIN Framework

The AI-DARWIN framework is a hybrid AI mechanistic model discovery tool designed to uncover interpretable and scientifically valid models from data. Unlike traditional black-box approaches, AI-DARWIN focuses on creating explainable models, ensuring that the resulting mathematical representations are not only accurate but also comprehensible to domain experts. This characteristic makes it particularly suitable for hybrid modeling tasks, such as those encountered in biomanufacturing processes. The framework operates by constraining the model discovery process to specific functional forms, such as polynomials. These constraints allow the generated models to remain aligned with physical laws and domain knowledge, fostering trust and usability in critical applications. By imposing such restrictions, AI-DARWIN ensures that the final models capture essential relationships without unnecessary complexity, making them easier to analyze and validate. For the Lovastatin biomanufacturing process, AI-DARWIN is applied to time-series data generated from the KTB1 mechanistic model. The framework systematically evaluates the dataset to uncover explicit mathematical relationships between input variables, such as nutrient concentrations (e.g., lactose and adenine), and output variables, such as Lovastatin API production. Moreover, normalization using standardization (subtracting the mean of the training data and dividing the difference by the standard deviation of the training data) was used to ensure that all input variables get weighted appropriately In the context of model optimization and system identification, several frameworks aim to extract meaningful insights from data. One such approach is SINDy [6]. While SINDy is not entirely one-shot (first step of parameter estimation is followed by iteratively sparsifying the coefficients), it comes up with a single best-fit model. AIDARWIN comes up with multiple options which allow for a broader range of discovery of functional relations between the input variables. This is particularly useful for systems biology models and surrogate models of complex systems. This also can provide a pareto front for further examination of the trade-off between simpler but less accurate models in contrast to complex but more accurate models. Further, AI-DARWIN provides flexibility to incorporate domain-specific constraints, which while limiting in accuracy, reaps rewards in interpretability and eventual robustness over unseen data. The resulting models are expressed in polynomial form, offering a clear interpretation of how each factor influences production outcomes. The generated datasets were used to train the models, with performance assessed using accuracy metrics such as Mean Absolute Error (MAE) and interpretability metrics like R². For each dataset, we conducted a series of Time (hour)

## 3. RESULTS AND DISCUSSION

For each dataset several model architectures were used, and the performance is compared. Table 1 and 2 show selected models developed to predict the concentration of two substrates consumed and the API produced at the outlet of the plant. The results demonstrate that the developed modeling framework effectively captures the complex dynamics of the Lovastatin production plant with low training and testing MAE values, demonstrating high predictive accuracy. Using a combination of mechanistic principles and data-driven modeling, the framework successfully predicts three critical outlet concentrations—C,AD, C,LAC, and C,LOV—at the plant's downstream stage. For Dataset 1, the models showed strong performance and the minimal error difference between training and testing for all the models suggests strong generalization (Table 1 and Figure 4). The polynomial models, expressed in interpretable forms, highlighted significant contributions from key variables such as ‘Q,R’ and ‘C,LA,1’ for predicting adenine concentration, and ‘Q,R’, ‘RF’, ‘C,X,4’, and ‘C,AD,P’ for predicting lactose and lovastatin concentrations. These terms reflect critical process interactions and nonlinearities inherent in this biomanufacturing system. Adenine concentration in stream 10 (C,AD), being the simplest model, achieves remarkable accuracy (MAEtest=0.0198), indicating that minimal complexity may suffice for certain targets. In contrast, lactose and lovastatin concentrations (C,LA and C,LOV) include higher-order terms like (C,X,44) and (C,AD,P4), reflecting more intricate relationships. These terms suggest significant nonlinearity in the system, consistent with the expected process dynamics. The inclusion of interaction terms, such as (C,X,4)(C,AD,P2), highlights interdependencies between process variables, offering deeper insights into their combined effects on product concentrations. While more complex models (e.g., C,LA and C,LOV) slightly improve accuracy, they may require more computational resources compared to C,AD. The models developed using Dataset 2 retained this interpretability while incorporating additional interactions and higher-order terms (Table 2 and Figure 5). For example, the C,LA model included interactions like (RF2),(C,X,42), and (Q,R2), emphasizing the interdependencies between upstream and downstream variables. Similarly, the C,LOV model highlighted the cascading influence of intermediate concentrations, such as C,LA,P on the final product. The similarity between the MAEs for training and testing sets is due to the relatively large dataset and the low model complexity, which together reduce the risk of overfitting. The large dataset and high sampling

However, this sampling strategy reflects realistic process monitoring where frequent measurements are common. The consistent performance across both subsets suggests that the model captures the underlying process behavior without overfitting. Nevertheless, we agree that further validation under different operating conditions would provide a stronger confirmation of generalizability.

### Figure 6 presents the sensitivity analysis performed

using MAPE. In Dataset 1, the C,AD,P point's distance from the center of the radar chart, represented by the blue line, indicates the highest sensitivity, meaning minor changes in C,AD,P significantly alter the MAPE. Although C,AD,P remain highly influential in Dataset 2 (magenta line), their impact is slightly reduced, which may be attributed to the overall improved fitting performance of the model. Similarly, C,X,4 exhibited significant sensitivity in both datasets, though to a lesser extent than C,AD,P, with Dataset 1 showing a marginally higher sensitivity than Dataset 2. Conversely, RF, Q,R, and C,LA,P demonstrated considerably lower sensitivity in both datasets. Despite slight variations in magnitude, the overall sensitivity patterns regarding biomass concentration were consistent across both datasets, with Dataset 1 showing a slightly higher sensitivity to changes in C,X,4. However, discrepancies in the sensitivities of RF and C,AD,P may arise from the fact that these represent two scenarios modeled for different data conditions (step changes in lactose and adenine, respectively). This modeling framework demonstrates its capability to model a plantwide biomanufacturing process, laying the groundwork for developing digital twins. The interpretability of the polynomial models facilitates understanding the underlying system dynamics, while the inclusion of complex interactions and nonlinear terms ensures the accuracy needed to capture the intricacies of the process. For models involving biomass concentration at stream 4 (C,X,4 after the fermentation stage) the kinetic models in the KTB1 model can be replaced to calculate biomass concentration in the CSTR for new microorganisms. By transferring the process dynamics knowledge captured in the polynomial models to new scenarios for different products, modeling of new scenarios can also be performed. This idea will be explored in future work, and validation will be required. Moreover, in the presented study, step changes in lactose and adenine concentrations—representing typical faults encountered in industrial processes—are introduced to the process. Within a broader digital twin framework that includes a fault diagnosis module, once faults are detected and diagnosed, the resulting changes in Lovastatin concentration at the output can be effectively estimated. This estimation can be done using key

### Figure 4 Model performance on the dataset 1

### Figure 5. Model performance on the dataset 2

Shahhoseyni et al. / LAPSE:2025.0422 Syst Control Trans 4:1682-1687 (2025) 1687 process variables, such as flow rates (directly measurable) or biomass concentration (easily calculable), by the models proposed in this study. The biomass concentration can be approximated through a kinetics model within the CSTR. This approach provides an opportunity for the plant to predict the final product concentration—after several units—using operational parameters (recycling factor and flow rate) along with the biomass concentration measured after the fermenter (via the kinetics model) beside nutrient concentrations after the hydro-cyclone.

### Figure 6. Sensitivity analysis of the models developed

for lovastatin production using both datasets.

## 4. CONCLUSION

The polynomial models developed in this study accurately capture the complex dynamics of a plantwide bio-manufacturing process, making the framework a valuable tool for advancing digital twins. By incorporating higher-order and interaction terms, the models effectively represent nonlinear relationships and interactions between process variables, enabling predictions under varying operational conditions. The low MAE values for both training and testing datasets indicate that the models generalize well, balancing accuracy and robustness. Their explicit functional forms provide interpretability, allowing for insight into variable sensitivities and interactions, which is essential for developing digital twins. Future work will focus on generating more complex datasets by varying multiple variables simultaneously, providing a richer dataset for model development. Additionally, testing more advanced models to ensure the selection of the best, yet simplest, representation would further validate this framework. This approach, coupled with a model selection framework, will optimize the trade-off between accuracy, complexity, and interpretability, expanding the applicability of the models across diverse scenarios. By using polynomial models to determine optimal process setpoints, we can adapt the system for different scenarios, such as changing the microorganism to produce a new product, and transfer insights to new contexts. Additionally, generating natural language explanations from the obtained ML plantwide models by incorporating domain-specific knowledge alongside large language models (LLMs) is part of the vision for future works. This approach could lead to the development of a large knowledge model (LKM) [7] tailored for biomanufacturing. This enhances the interoperability of our obtained models and can be used to generate additional mechanistic insights.

# Hybrid Modeling for Bioprocesses: Architectures, Applications, and Perspectives

Engineering Reports REVIEW OPEN ACCESS Hybrid Modeling for Bioprocesses: Architectures, Applications, and Perspectives Juan Federico Herrera-Ruiz | Javier Fontalvo | Oscar Andrés Prado-Rubio Grupo de Investigación en Aplicación de Nuevas Tecnologías (GIANT) Departamento de Ingeniería Química, Universidad Nacional de Colombia Sede Manizales, Campus La Nubia, Manizales, Colombia Correspondence: Oscar Andrés Prado-Rubio (oaprador@unal.edu.co) Received: 6 May 2025 | Revised: 7 October 2025 | Accepted: 5 November 2025 Keywords: bioprocesses engineering | hybrid modeling | semi-parametric modeling, modeling

## ABSTRACT

Recent hardware developments within industrial digitalization make more evident the need of high-fidelity models to exploit process system engineering potential in biomanufacturing. Hybrid models, which combine first-principles (white-box) and data-driven (black-box) approaches, aim to leverage the strengths of both methodologies to mitigate their individual limitations to provide high predictive power. This article explores the integration of hybrid modeling techniques in bioprocess engineering, emphasizing their potential to facilitate product development, accelerate experimental design, enhance process monitoring, drive optimization and catalyze the implementation of digitalization strategies to achieve a more robust and sustainable production in the framework of the goals set by the UN 2030 agenda. In this review, we analyze 270 publications from the early 1990s to 2024, extracting application domains, hybrid-model architectures, implementation software, and nonparametric output structures. Our findings highlight marked improvements in predictive accuracy, and extrapolation capabilities while revealing persistent challenges in integrating mechanistic and empirical components. This review on hybrid modeling implementations shows their potential to enhance accuracy, calibration, and extrapolation capabilities, highly required for bioprocesses. This work underscores the importance of integrating mechanistic and data-driven approaches to improve the flexibility and interpretability of models in complex engineering systems, offering significant implications for more sustainable and innovative bioprocesses. 1 | Introduction Sustainability has become a critical focus in addressing global challenges, as it ensures that current development meets the needs of present generations without compromising the ability of future generations to meet their own needs. The Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs), set by the United Nations, provide a comprehensive framework to tackle pressing issues such as climate change and environmental degradation. Among these, goals like Industry, Innovation and Infrastructure (SDG9), Responsible Consumption and Production (SDG 12), Climate ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- This is an open access article under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution License, which permits use, distribution and reproduction in any medium, provided the original work is properly cited. © 2025 The Author(s). Engineering Reports published by John Wiley & Sons Ltd. Action (SDG 13), and Life Below Water (SDG 14) are directly impacted by how industries operate, making sustainable practices essential [1]. As of 2024, the SDGs were in a dire situation, with most of them being severely delayed and targets not on track to being met [1]. For SDG 9, less than 20% of the targets were on track to being met, whereas for SDG 12 the share was around 30%. Moreover, the UN estimates that 84% of the SDG targets have either stagnated or shown a reversal of progress. This indicates that further acceleration is needed to achieve the targets. Research in diverse Engineering Reports, 2025; 7:e70502 1 of 30 https://doi.org/10.1002/eng2.70502 areas of knowledge and articulation with industry is crucial to meet the 2030 agenda [1]. In the framework of the above-mentioned goals, biorefineries emerge as an attractive approach to achieve them. Second and third generation biorefineries (2G and 3G) allow biomass and agricultural wastes to take full potential, without compromising food security, which in turn makes them very attractive for adding value to waste [2]. Bioprocesses offer a promising avenue to meet sustainability goals by reducing reliance on fossil fuels and lowering carbon footprints [3]. However, they also pose significant challenges, including the complexity of biological systems and the need to balance economic viability with ecological responsibility [4]. Moreover, biorefineries are often complex processes that involve many steps, posing a technical challenge from their inception through the implementation. One of the main challenges of biorefineries takes place in the bioreaction stage. Biotransformations are intricate, and their mechanisms are usually not well understood, which leads to low titer, productivity, and yield. Process systems engineering (PSE) plays a vital role in addressing these challenges by integrating knowledge from engineering, mathematics, and computer science to optimize bioprocesses [5]. PSE methods enable the design, control, and operation of bioprocess systems in a way that improves efficiency and minimizes environmental impact. For instance, through advanced modeling, simulation, and optimization techniques, PSE can help predict the behavior of biological systems, optimize resource usage, and identify bottlenecks in production [6, 7]. In PSE, modeling takes a central role for process simulation and its use [8, 9]. Models can administrate information from two main perspectives: structural (our understanding of the underlying mechanisms) and the model parametrization (the nature of the numeric techniques used to describe the relationships between data). From the structural point of view, first-principles, mechanistic or phenomenological models represent a wide class of models, usually called white-box, due to being developed primarily on conservation equations and physical laws. First principles models are constructed through deductive reasoning, wherein the model equations are derived from existing knowledge of the system, using established relationships such as conservation laws. Mechanistic models usually comprise a system of differential algebraic equations (DAEs). An important characteristic of mechanistic models is that they can be extrapolated to process conditions outside of the calibration space, thanks in part to being developed from conservation laws, although this is not always the case [10]. In contrast, data-driven models typically employ an inductive approach, wherein a generic mathematical model is fitted to map inputs to outputs, without considering the intrinsic structure of the phenomena, unlike their white-box counterparts [10–12]. Data-driven techniques usually take the form of arbitrary functions to model the relations between inputs and outputs. Gray models usually fall in between, combining mechanistic knowledge and data-driven techniques [13, 14]. From another perspective, models can be classified regarding the form of model parametrization. Parametric models are established beforehand based on the knowledge of the process [15]. These models possess a finite number of parameters, often characterized by physical significance, which vary according to the model’s complexity [11]. In contrast, nonparametric models are solely derived from data, lacking predefined parameters [15]. The term “nonparametric” does not mean that the model is built without parameters, but that the number of them is variable, not fixed in advance by knowledge, and that they commonly lack physical significance [11]. In nonparametric modeling, mathematical techniques such as algebraic, series expansions, machine learning (ML), and other black-box approaches are used to derive insights from inputs to outputs. These techniques are also called “surrogate models” when they are used to approximate the behavior of more complex and computationally costly models [16] and are used to describe the relevant process behavior exclusively from model-generated data, to perform calculations faster such as optimization [17, 18]. Typically, they have great predicting power inside the space of their calibration, but extrapolation is not advisable [19]. For a better understanding of the advantages and disadvantages of both parametric and nonparametric modeling, Table 1 shows a comparison between parametric and nonparametric modeling [20–27].

### Table 1, presents advantages and limitations between parametric and nonparametric modeling [20–27]. Parametric models are

built based on prior knowledge of the system, incorporating physical laws and constraints, which makes them highly interpretable and ensures their compliance with conservation laws. They have low variability, making them more robust and reliable, especially when data is scarce. Parametric models exhibit strong extrapolation capabilities, allowing them to make reasonable predictions beyond the range of the observed data. However, their reliance on predefined equations introduces a strong bias, which may lead to inaccurate predictions if the assumed structure does

### TABLE 1

| Comparison between parametric and nonparametric modeling [20–27]. Parametric modeling Nonparametric modeling Model variability Low High Model bias High Low Model parameter data required High Low Mechanism understanding required High Low Model flexibility Medium High Explainability and interpretability High Low Interpolation capabilities Medium High Extrapolation capacities Good Bad Computational training cost Low High Risk of overfitting Low High Compliance with conservation laws High Low 2 of 30 Engineering Reports, 2025 25778196, 2025, 12, Downloaded from https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/eng2.70502, Wiley Online Library on [13/05/2026]. See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License not fully capture the system dynamics. Developing parametric models requires extensive domain expertise, making the process time-intensive and complex, often involving experts from multiple disciplines. Nonparametric models offer greater flexibility as they do not require prior mechanistic knowledge, making them particularly useful when the underlying system is poorly understood or difficult to describe mathematically. These models can efficiently capture complex, nonlinear relationships within data, often uncovering hidden patterns that parametric models might miss. However, their flexibility comes at a cost. Nonparametric models typically require large amounts of high-quality data for training, and their computational cost is significantly higher, particularly during the training phase. Additionally, they are prone to overfitting and overparameterization, making them less robust, and they often struggle with extrapolation beyond the observed data range. They are usually not reliable enough to extrapolate to conditions outside of their calibration space; however, they have great interpolation capabilities within it. Another critical drawback is their lack of interpretability, as they do not rely on physical constraints or conservation laws, which can make it difficult to understand the underlying mechanisms driving predictions. From previous experience, there is no ideal model, with both parametric and nonparametric models having strengths and shortcomings that not only depend on system understanding, but also on data and computational power. As a potential trade-off, the scientific community has proposed hybrid modeling, also called hybrid semi-parametric modeling, to overcome the challenges that arise from parametric and nonparametric modeling, while retaining most of their advantages. Notice that researchers do not always agree on the definition and use of the term “hybrid model.” Some authors claim to have developed hybrid models, when they combine different mechanistic and empiric knowledge (mathematical relationships that provide reliable approximations but are not built using mechanistic knowledge) such as correlations for heat and mass transfer coefficients, for example, [28–34]. Besides, the term has been used when modified structures apply to pure nonparametric approaches for predictions. These modifications usually entail combinations of artificial neural networks (ANNs) with other ML techniques or optimization algorithms [35–40]. Finally, “hybrid modeling” has been applied to other approaches such as Computational Fluid Dynamics applications that integrate various numerical methods [41] and process system engineering simulations that use multiple software tools to perform the simulations [42–44]. Here in, “hybrid modeling” represents a modeling paradigm that seeks to combine the strengths of both parametric and nonparametric modeling while minimizing their individual disadvantages. Hybrid modeling is an emerging approach gaining traction; thus there are recent reviews in the literature detailing the applications of hybrid modeling to separation processes [45], biochemical processes using neural networks [24, 46, 47], wastewater treatment (WTT) [48]; chemical, petroleum and energy systems [46, 49]. For bioprocesses specifically, recent reviews include topics such as the role of hybrid models in bioprocess scale-up [50]; the applications of hybrid modeling to systems biology for mammalian cell culture development [51], and the prospects of hybrid modeling for industry 4.0 and 5.0 [20, 23]. Those contributions delve into the details of relevant discussions. However, they lack quantitative and qualitative trends and information on how hybrid models have been implemented in bioprocesses. This study provides the main characteristics of hybrid models, such as structure, knowledge integration, performance assessment, data requirements, and challenges. This contribution focuses on the hybrid modeling of reactions in bioprocesses, aggregating information from 270 contributions screened down from 5890 identified from a bibliometric search. From there, the target is to identify trends such as the types of bioprocesses to which hybrid modeling has been applied, the preferred architectures/structures/design patterns, the most used nonparametric techniques, the scale of the experimental data used to develop the models, the mode of operation of the experiments, and software used for building the models, as well as cross-correlations between these categories. This study describes the literature review methodology in Section 2, Section 3 presents the general characteristics of hybrid semi-parametric models, whereas Section 4 shows insights and correlations of the different implementations of hybrid models to bioprocesses, including an overview of some recent applications of hybrid semi-parametric models to bioprocesses. Finally, Section 5 presents conclusions, current challenges, and future perspectives. 2 | Literature Review Methodology A comprehensive bibliometric study was performed to quantitatively and qualitatively map the research landscape using descriptors such as journals, publication outputs, institutions, authors, and keywords. Full details of the methodology for the literature review can be found elsewhere [52]. Here, a summary is presented. The search strategy began with identifying the need for information, focusing on hybrid modeling applications in bioprocesses. Non-biotechnological processes were excluded to maintain the relevance of the field. Scopus and Web of Science were selected as the primary databases due to their extensive and complementary coverage of scientific literature. A search equation was designed by expanding terms, incorporating keywords related to hybrid models (e.g., “hybrid model,” “hybrid neural network”) and bioprocesses (e.g., “bio*,” “bioproc*”). The search was restricted to publications from 1992, marking a seminal work in the field, through March 2024. The search was repeated on December 2024 to encompass new publications. Search results were refined by targeting specific fields: “TitleAbstract-Keywords” for Scopus and “Topics” for Web of Science. Abstracts were validated manually to ensure relevance to Chemical and Biochemical Engineering, excluding unrelated documents. This iterative process included refining the search equation multiple times, guided by insights gained during analysis. The search continued until results stabilized, with less than 5% variation in top authors, journals, and total results across iterations. A total of 5890 contributions were found, and after a manual review, 270 contributions were selected as of Engineering Reports, 2025 3 of 30 25778196, 2025, 12, Downloaded from https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/eng2.70502, Wiley Online Library on [13/05/2026]. See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License interest. Information was refined using software like VantagePoint, VOSViewer, Bibliometrix, and Python. Later, a manual assessment of each contribution was performed. 3 | Hybrid Modeling Framework This section is divided into subsections to provide a clear and comprehensive look at hybrid modeling. Section 3.1 introduces the general characteristics of hybrid models, laying the groundwork for understanding their key features. Section 3.2 dives into the different structures commonly used to implement these models, whereas Section 3.3 explores various ways to incorporate knowledge into their design. Section 3.4 highlights some of the most popular nonparametric substructures used in hybrid modeling for bioprocesses. Section 3.5 focuses on how the performance of these models has been assessed, followed by Section 3.6, which discusses the data requirements necessary for building them, and lastly, Section 3.7 showcases some of the main applications of hybrid models. 3.1 | Main Characteristics of Hybrid Modeling In the middle of the parametric and nonparametric modeling paradigms, the called “hybrid models” or “hybrid semiparametric models” exist, combining both first principles and data-driven techniques to describe the systems. Figure 1 shows the knowledge basis and mathematical representations for parametric, nonparametric, and hybrid modeling. Parametric models heavily rely on first principles and prior knowledge, using conservation laws, mass-energy-momentum balances, kinetic mechanisms, thermodynamic relationships, and transport laws to describe the system. Parametric models are usually expressed as algebraic equations, DAEs or partial differential equations (PDEs), with data being mostly used for parameter identification (hence the dotted green line in Figure 1). Nonparametric modeling approaches are usually data-hungry [23, 26, 45, 50] and build their knowledge exclusively from process data, extracting no information from first principles. Nonparametric models are usually expressed as data-driven techniques such as ANNs, Gaussian Processes (GPs), and support vector machines (SVMs) or Autoregressive models (ARX, ARMAX, ARIMA). Hybrid modeling aims to compromise both approaches in the middle, needing fewer data than nonparametric models, but without requiring prior knowledge as extensive as parametric models do. Hybrid models can be expressed in a wide range of structures, mixing the data-driven techniques used for nonparametric modeling and the mechanistic knowledge expressed in parametric models. It is important to note that hybrid models do not necessarily use their parametric and nonparametric components in the same ratio. Thus, the spectrum seen in Figure 1 signals that hybrid models can be “darker” if they rely more on the nonparametric techniques than the mechanistic ones, or “lighter” if the inverse is true. Literature often claims that hybrid modeling leverages most of the advantages of parametric and nonparametric modeling while minimizing its disadvantages. Although this is empirically seen in an increased performance when compared against full mechanistic models, there are sparse mathematical proofs. It has been proven that giving more structure to the nonparametric subcomponent via an additive term helps to reduce the asymptotic variance of the parametric parameters [53]. Authors prove that if the parametric model is ill conditioned, hybrid models will outperform it. Furthermore, a Focused Information Criterion is

### FIGURE 1

| Parametric, nonparametric, and hybrid semi-parametric modeling and the types of knowledge they are based on. Adapted from [11]. 4 of 30 Engineering Reports, 2025 25778196, 2025, 12, Downloaded from https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/eng2.70502, Wiley Online Library on [13/05/2026]. See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License proposed, showing that in small data samples hybrid models are heavily reliant on induced bias from parametric ones, but if the data has low variance, the hybrid model enhances prediction capabilities requiring less data than a full nonparametric approach [54]. Hybrid modeling main characteristics are [25, 26, 49, 55]:

## 1. Hybrid models usually present better parameter estimation,

due to: a. Less parameters than pure nonparametric techniques, which can lead to higher statistical confidence of the identified parameters. b. The chance to map all measurable quantities as inputs to the model structure, where the relationships are established using the nonparametric sub model. Thus, more information is included than in parametric models, usually allowed due to deficiencies in process understanding. c. Combining data-driven approaches with mechanistic knowledge results in a constrained space that imposes physical meaning and bounds to the variables, which is hard to achieve in pure non-parametrical techniques. d. When compared to pure nonparametric models, hybrid modeling exhibits superior calibration properties and enhanced extrapolation properties when compared to pure nonparametric techniques due to the structuring of the operation space using mechanistic or phenomenological knowledge. This structuring reduces the search space, consequently requiring less data for training hybrid models than for pure nonparametric approaches. However, the strategies for training and validation of hybrid models are heavily dependent on the model structure/architecture chosen.

## 2. Less mechanistic or first principles knowledge is needed for

developing a hybrid model than a complex pure parametric model.

## 3. Hybrid models are usually developed faster than pure

parametric models, since less mechanistic knowledge is required. However, they are highly dependent on quality data and its availability. 4. The mechanistic knowledge enhances the model interpretability, making it more “transparent” than nonparametric models (hence the term “gray box model” is also used). 3.2 | Hybrid Model Design Patterns Recently, a comprehensive overview of design patterns for hybrid models was presented [26]. These design patterns generalize, unify, and formalize diverse modeling approaches across various domains and applications. The following sections introduce these patterns and illustrate them through selected relevant studies from the literature. Notice that the chemical engineering literature has used the term “architecture” [11, 23, 56]. Recently, a distinction between “design pattern” and “architecture” was proposed because any model architecture can have different combinations of different design patterns, working in parallel, in series or nested inside another [26]. In the present work, “architecture” and “design pattern” are synonyms, whereas “structure” is used to discuss the different combinations and arrangements of the design patterns. It is important to note that a model structure can comprise one or several design patterns. Agarwal [56] presented the general architectures in which the white box and black box components of a hybrid model can be arranged. Figure 2 shows three configurations. Configurations (a) and (b) show a serial architecture, where a black box output serves as input for the white box, and the white box output serves as input for the black box, respectively. Configuration (c) shows a parallel architecture, and finally, configuration (d) shows a data-driven technique constrained by a parametric model. Configurations (a), (b), (c), and (d) are also called “feature learning,” “physics-based preprocessing,” “delta model,” and “physical constraints,” respectively [26]. In Figure 2 there are two types of inputs for configurations (a), (b), and (c), named x1 and x2 (color coded blue and green, respectively) to clarify that the inputs for the different subcomponents of hybrid models might be different, but also could include common variables.

### FIGURE 2

| Types of design patterns used for building hybrid models: (a) feature learning; (b) physics-based preprocessing; (c) delta model; (d) physical constraints Adapted from [11, 26]. Engineering Reports, 2025 5 of 30 25778196, 2025, 12, Downloaded from https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/eng2.70502, Wiley Online Library on [13/05/2026]. See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License The design pattern choice not only influences the interaction between the different components of a hybrid model, but also determines the training strategy and, therefore, computational cost of developing a model. 3.2.1 | Serial Architectures The most popular hybrid model architecture is the “feature learning” design pattern. In this architecture, the white box is usually composed of conservation laws, mass, heat, or momentum transfer relations that describe the process. The black box typically represents the kinetics of the process or the transport coefficients, for which there is no general valid model [10, 47, 57]. This architecture is very useful when there is no precise knowledge of the underlying mechanisms, but there is enough data to identify the underlying patterns. Another scenario is presented when there is a huge amount of data that can be exploited to model the underlying mechanisms [58, 59]. The “physics-based preprocessing” design pattern is mostly used in data sparse environments, primarily using data augmentation [12]. In this architecture, the white-box model predictions are used to establish a link between the state of the system and certain parameters [11]. Some examples include: the prediction of cell culture using ANNs [60] where authors used an ANN to model some reaction rates in their dynamic balance flux model using other mechanistic reaction rates and states and inputs to the data-driven components, a fed-batch process for producing penicillin [61] taking advantage of Kalman filters to estimate and reconstruct some states from noisy data and predict fault detections, the control of biomass in protein production using Pichia pastoris [62] implementing a soft-sensor to predict biomass concentration based on online measurements of CO2 utilizing a multi-linear regression that takes advantage of the mechanistic carbon balance. When utilizing serial architectures, the training can be done in two different ways: coupling the identification of the parametric and nonparametric components simultaneously or decoupling the identification, usually calibrating a parametric structure and then calibrating a nonparametric component to account for the deficiencies of the parametric component. 3.2.2 | Parallel Architecture The parallel architecture (“delta model” design pattern) applies if there is a process model (white box) that captures the underlying mechanisms but has limited predictive power due to structural factors not accounted for (unmodeled effects, nonlinearities, dynamic behavior) [26]. The predictive power of the model can be enhanced using nonparametric techniques, although their performance remains poor outside of the training region [11, 23]. This structure is interesting if the system can be decoupled into non-interacting parts, that is, static non-linear and dynamic linear behavior. This modeling approach has been applied to cases such as predicting phosphorus removal in a WTT process [63] correcting the model’s mismatch by adding the output of an ANN to the mechanistic reaction rates to enhance prediction capabilities; using Kalman filters to correct bias from mechanistic model predictions and enabling higher forecasting capabilities in each integration time in ethanol fermentations for biofuel production [64]; using ANNs to correct the predictions of parametric models in cell apoptosis [65]; applying corrections to the activated sludge model in an industrial cokes wastewater-plant [66] correcting the activated sludge model by using five different data-driven techniques like ANNs and multiple linear regressions; predicting biomass concentration in a baker’s yeast fermentation [67] utilizing a mixture of experts, where each data-driven component is used to predict the states and a gating function combines them for enhancing prediction capabilities; studying polymerization processes by simplifying the parametric structure of the model [68] or fed-batch penicillin fermentations [15] where simplified models are proposed and the data-driven techniques take full advantage of residuals and state predictions to have similar performance to more computationally demanding models. Researchers have proposed three main ways for approaching the parallel structure, shown in Figure 3 [11]. The (a) configuration is called “superposition” where the black box output is summed or subtracted from the white box model prediction to enhance the model performance. This configuration works as a bias correction, changing the output variance and shape. This is the most used parallel structure, first proposed in bioprocesses to study a fed-batch penicillin fermentation [15]. Configuration (b) is called “multiplication” because the output of the model is the multiplication of the outputs predicted by the black box and white box, working as a correction factor (amplification or mitigation of the parametric outputs). It was first utilized in bioprocesses for a bakers’ yeast production process [17]. The (c) configuration is applied when the outputs of the black box and white box are transformed by either a black box or a white box model [69], as such, weight factors (w1 and w2) are applied to combine them and obtain the final prediction. It is important to note that the inputs to the nonparametric and parametric parts could be different (as evidenced in Figure 3); therefore, the nonparametric component might obtain knowledge from information that the parametric models do not consider in their formulation. 3.2.3 | Physical Constraints Physical constraints represent a hybrid modeling design pattern that integrates domain knowledge—such as conservation laws—into the structure, parameters, or outputs of a data-driven model [20, 26, 70]. These constraints can be categorized as hard constraints and soft constraints, based on how strictly they are enforced. • Hard constraints encode physical principles directly into the model’s structure, ensuring they are never violated. For instance, adding a ReLU (rectified linear unit function, which is a function that is 0 when its input is less than or equal to 0 and is a linear function when its input is greater than 0) activation layer to guarantee non-negative outputs for variables like concentrations or pressure, or using a softmax layer to ensure molar fractions sum to one, are common examples [71]; or using Neural ODEs to predict ethanol production via fermentation [72] or Chinese Hamster Ovary cells (CHO) biomass growth in a bioreactor [73]. However, hard constraints require the input data to align with the 6 of 30 Engineering Reports, 2025 25778196, 2025, 12, Downloaded from https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/eng2.70502, Wiley Online Library on [13/05/2026]. See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License

### FIGURE 3

| Types of design pattern architecture. (a) linear superposition; (b) multiplication; (c) weighed predictions by using either a black box or a white box. The individual predictions of white boxes are denoted as b(x2) while for black boxes they are denoted as a(x1). In configuration (c), the individual predictions are added using weighting factors (w1 and w2). Adapted from [11]. imposed rules, a process known as data reconciliation [20]. A challenge with hard constraints is that they can result in non-differentiable models complicating gradient-based optimization methods [26]. • Soft constraints incorporate mechanistic insights into the loss function as penalties. These penalties guide the model during training to produce outputs that align with domain knowledge, allowing some flexibility in how strictly the constraints are followed [20, 26]. Once training is complete, the model relies solely on the data-driven architecture, with the domain knowledge encoded implicitly. Physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) are an example of soft-constrained models [70]. PINNs have been successfully applied to simulated cases of bioprocesses [74, 75]; kinetics of biodiesel production [76]; and WTT plants [75]. When using hard constraints at the output, the results are guaranteed to be physically plausible; however, these constraints can introduce non-differentiable nonlinearities that complicate gradient-based optimization. To address this, soft constraints are sometimes used because they provide a smoother optimization landscape, even though they do not guarantee exact adherence to the desired conditions. Moreover, combining multiple physical constraints in a single model can be challenging. A key benefit of incorporating physical constraints is enhanced data efficiency. By embedding prior knowledge into the model, the complexity of the problem can be reduced, which in turn lowers the amount of training data needed [26]. 3.3 | Knowledge Integration The standard way to incorporate mechanistic knowledge into a hybrid model is using mathematical expressions such as conservation laws or underlying phenomena. But they are not the only form of knowledge that can be incorporated. Some examples are: • Knowledge about different stages of a process (e.g., growth phase; lag phase; etc. for a fermentation) can be leveraged to apply fuzzy logic (FL) either to enhance prediction for each phase by changing the mechanistic model [77–80] or to change the structure of ANNs [81]. • Design of experiments can evaluate and optimize the black box structure [82]. • Constraints can be added to the output predictions of the hybrid model to enhance its performance [25]. • Physics laws can be encoded in the training phase of the black-box component as in Physics Informed Neural Networks [47]. Mechanistic knowledge should be incorporated only if it is highly certain, which means that it does not contain empirical assumptions to avoid producing a model with high bias. The white box should be limited to knowledge that represents the physical interactions of the system [25]. For example, mass balances can be incorporated without hesitation, as the mass conservation laws apply to all systems. Assumptions like lumped parameters in a Engineering Reports, 2025 7 of 30 25778196, 2025, 12, Downloaded from https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/eng2.70502, Wiley Online Library on [13/05/2026]. See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License chemostat might not always hold true, especially in large systems; therefore, the assumption introduces bias to the model. The unknown elements of a hybrid model, such as kinetic terms, unknown or unmeasured variables, or mass transfer coefficients are usually represented by a nonparametric structure. 3.4 | Data Driven Components of Hybrid Models Since the beginning of the century, the increasing availability of data, driven by the continuous advancement of better and more precise sensors, larger databases, and enhanced computational power, has compelled engineers to recognize data as a valuable source of information [12]. Therefore, mathematical techniques that build insights from data are highly valuable. Non-parametric and data-driven techniques can be broadly classified in the following categories, with accompanying examples of their applications to bioprocesses: • Algebraic expressions such as polynomials or rational functions for predicting the pH and acetic acid production of Kombucha fermentations [83], and sigmoidal and exponential functions for describing methane production during the anaerobic digestion of orange peel waste [84]. • Series expressions such as Fourier series used for optimizing the pH profile for controlling the process of the yeast Wickerhamomyces anomalus against the spoilage yeast Zygosaccharomyces rouxii in wine pre-fermentation [85]. • Statistical models such as principal component analysis (PCA) for identifying the correlations between variables for monoclonal antibodies production [86], and multivariate data analysis (MVDA) for predicting the production of adenovirus using human embryonic kidney cells (HEK 293) [87]. • Time-series autoregressive approaches such as ARMAX for controlling dissolved oxygen in a S. cerevisiae fermentation [88], ARIMA for modeling and predicting a membrane bioreactor (MBR) for treating ampicillin from pharmaceutical wastewater [89] or ARX for predicting biomass concentrations in a lactic acid fermentation [90]. • ML approaches such as ANNs, as well as techniques like GPs or SVMs [91]. An overview of several nonparametric structures such as ANNs, Random Forests (RF), SVMs, GPs, among others has been recently presented [92]. The choice of nonparametric technique coupled with the hybrid model’s structure heavily influences the development and the strategy for training and validation of the model. 3.5 | Hybrid Model Performance Assessment Hybrid models’ performance and extrapolation capabilities are directly related to the structure of the nonparametric portion of the model. This refers not only to whether the architecture is parallel or serial but also to the configuration and the specific model structure of the nonparametric model, particularly its size (e.g., layers of an ANN, number of neurons per layer, type of activation function, etc.) [11]. The size of the data-driven technique can be assessed using the Akaike Information Criteria (AIC) or Bayesian Information Criteria (BIC), the latter being preferred for models with a high number of parameters [55, 57]. For discriminating between models, Friedman’s test can be applied [93]. In general, there should be a trade-off between estimation quality and the number of parameters, to avoid overfitting or underfitting issues, which should also be addressed in training and validation. To compare hybrid models’ performance to traditional parametric and nonparametric models, researchers have also used more conventional metrics such as the coefficient of determination r2 despite not being suited for nonlinear estimators in biochemical systems [94], the mean squared error (MSE) and its derivatives such as root mean square error (RMSE) or normalized MSE (NMSE), error measurements such as mean absolute percentage error (MAPE), mean absolute error (MAE), among others. When extrapolating the hybrid model’s predictions outside the training domain, some assessment of the validity of the extrapolation should be done. Authors have mainly used two complementary criteria: convex-hull criteria and a confidence interval criterion. The convex-hull criteria are used to verify that each empirical model solely interpolates the data gathered during model identification. Meanwhile, the confidence interval criterion evaluates the confidence intervals for the predictions of the hybrid model [95]. Other authors have proposed that the confidence interval of the hybrid model can be estimated using Bayesian optimization, resampling the model’s prediction using Markov Chain Monte Carlo simulations [96]. 3.6 | Hybrid Modeling Requirements for Data Data is essential for discerning the structure and parameters of the hybrid model, and principally all properties of the model (such as prediction quality and extrapolation capabilities) heavily rely both on the quality and quantity of the data [10, 12, 24]. Data is necessary to develop any hybrid model. If there is no previous data, it must be generated, with a systematic exploration of the design space (e.g., using Design of Experiments for a system or a benchmark model). Different techniques can be applied, such as factorial design, sequential design, optimal design, and model-based design methodologies [97]. A valuable alternative is the so-called “Intensified Design of Experiments,” where intra-experimental shifts to key parameters are performed to capture the dynamics of the process [59, 98, 99]. This methodology has been applied specifically to develop hybrid models, yielding great predictive power with a reduced number of experiments in an early stage of model development. Intensified design of experiments has been paired with hybrid models to transfer knowledge from lab scale to pilot scale in CHO cultures [100], reducing the number of experiments by as much as 50% while retaining the same levels of accuracy in monoclonal CHO line producing immunoglobulin G4 monoclonal antibody [101, 102], describing the design space with similar accuracy as a full-factorial design of experiments despite having less than 33% 8 of 30 Engineering Reports, 2025 25778196, 2025, 12, Downloaded from https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/eng2.70502, Wiley Online Library on [13/05/2026]. See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License of the experiments in an E. coli culture for recombinant protein production [103]. Regardless of the experimental design, data should be cleaned and properly pretreated before using it for hybrid modeling purposes. For example, small variances in feeding concentration can cause big errors in the estimation of substrate concentrations [57]. Hybrid model performance is affected by higher levels of noise, increasing the variance of the identified model parameters [104, 105]. Noise from data can be extracted using techniques such as outlier removal and signal filtering [106] or using surrogate models for data augmentation to increase model stability in predictions [23, 107, 108]. 3.7 | Hybrid Modeling Applications A classification according to the use given to the hybrid model has been proposed, providing in-depth examples for each category [12]. Based on that classification, the main uses for hybrid modeling in bioprocesses are shown in Table 2. Besides the previous applications, hybrid models have gained momentum for the development of digital shadows and digital twins, to accelerate Industry 4.0 and digitalization in chemical processes [16, 119]. In the following sections, insights regarding hybrid modeling of bioprocesses are presented. Due to the vast number of references analyzed, only a select number of them will be included in the text as examples. The database of papers analyzed will be made available on request. 4 | Hybrid Modeling Implementation Recently, a comprehensive bibliometric analysis [52] spanning 360 contributions revealed that the hybrid modeling of bioprocesses is a growing research area, with an annual growth rate of 9% since its inception in 1992. Recent advancements, particularly post-2017, underscore the impact of heightened awareness surrounding artificial intelligence and the integration of next-generation ML techniques into hybrid models. Key themes driving this field include “hybrid model,” “neural networks,” and applications ranging from fermentation to biopharmaceutical manufacturing. A keyword network analysis identified three central research clusters: process control applications, computational techniques, and the broader integration of bioprocessing terminology with ML concepts. This review builds on these findings, delving into quantifying trends, innovations, and challenges in hybrid modeling for bioprocesses, setting the stage for a comprehensive examination of the current state of the field to identify trends such as the types of hybrid modeling on bioprocesses, the preferred design patterns, the most used nonparametric techniques, the scale of the experimental data for developing the models, the mode of operation of the experiments, software used for building the models, as well as

### TABLE 2

| Main uses for hybrid modeling and some applications. Type of use Case study Process Monitoring • Predicting mixed liquor suspended solids, chemical oxygen demand, and nitrate concentration in the permeate of a membrane bioreactor for wastewater treatment [109] • Monitoring penicillin G acylase production by Bacillus megaterium using principal component analysis for detecting deviations from the desired trajectory [110]. Process Control • Applying Support Vector Regression and Universal Kalman Filters in a therapeutic protein production using E. coli [111] • Using online measurements such as pH, temperature and dissolved oxygen to control a target metabolite in a Bordetella pertussis cultivation [112] Process Optimization • Using ANNs in conjunction with the material balances to optimize the feeding strategy of a fed-batch bioreactor to maximize the yield of a protein [113], • Using ANNs and particle swarm optimization to determine the optimal conditions that maximize ethanol production in a fermentation by S. cerevisiae grown using cashew apple juice [114]. Scale-up • A hybrid model was developed using data from 300 mL flasks and applied it to describe the process in a 15 L bioreactor [100]. Analysis of model-plant mismatch • Determining the deviations and correcting the predictions of pH, chemical oxygen demand and other variables in a wastewater treatment plant [115], • Monitoring the soluble chemical oxygen demand for improving the nitrogen removal efficiency in a wastewater treatment plant [116]. Model Transfer • The use of meta-learning and one-hot encoding to transfer knowledge from one hybrid model to another process reducing test error when compared to a model trained only in the new experiments [117] • Using process data in one experimental condition to transfer the knowledge to another set of conditions, improving the behavior of the model [118]. Engineering Reports, 2025 9 of 30 25778196, 2025, 12, Downloaded from https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/eng2.70502, Wiley Online Library on [13/05/2026]. See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License current challenges and future potential. This section highlights selected examples to illustrate the main macro trends in the field, rather than providing a detailed account of all contributions. Section 4.1 presents an overview of the most used nonparametric substructures for hybrid models. Section 4.2 presents the types of processes that have been successfully modeled using hybrid semi-parametric approaches with the most used microorganisms in those processes. Section 4.3 presents the type, scale of the data and mode of operation of the processes used for hybrid modeling. Section 4.4 presents some computational details regarding the implementation of hybrid models, and finally Section 4.5 presents some recent trends. 4.1 | Data-Driven Substructures Model Approaches

### Figure 4 shows the distribution of the data-driven techniques

used for hybrid modeling. A total of 374 different hybrid model implementations were found in the literature. It’s important to note that there are more individual implementations than works analyzed because one work can have more than one implementation. ANNs dominate as the preferred black box component in hybrid modeling, accounting for 56.2% of the total modeling approaches. Inside the ANN set, 67% of the authors that use ANNs do not specify the type of ANN they use, for example, using ANNs to develop hybrid models for CHO cultivation from shake flask cultivations and yielding similar accuracy to the mechanistic model despite using only 4 of 33 experiments for training [120], or to describe a B. subtilis fermentation to maximize the spores produced [121]. The remaining authors have used the following types: multi-layered perceptron (MLP), which are 13% of the applications and have been used to describe the reaction rates in perfusion cultures of mammalian cells, reducing the elementary fluxes modeled from 22,563 to 5 [60], or to predict a CHO cultivation process by replacing reaction rates with MLPs [122], or to model the enzymatic kinetics for producing galactooligosaccharides using β-galactosidase from Kluyveromyces lactis [123]. Deep Neural Networks (DNN) comprise 4% of the total ANN applications and have been used to describe processes such as the production of glycoproteins in a CHO culture, resulting in an error 12% lower when compared to the shallow counterparts despite an increase in computation time of 31% [124], modeling the E. coli threonine synthesis [125], or modeling real industrial fermentations with a 75% reduction in RMSE [126]. Feedforward Neural Networks (FNN) which account for 3% of the total ANN applications, have been used to predict the gas composition of a biomass gasification process [127] or to predict the insulinase

### FIGURE 4

| Distribution of black box models’ categories and subcategories used by authors when implementing hybrid Models in bioprocesses. Bubble size indicates number of occurrences in the database. ANN, artificial neural networks; BPNN, backpropagation neural networks; DNN, deep neural networks; FL, fuzzy logic; FNN, feedforward neural networks; GA, genetic algorithms; GAN, generative adversarial neural networks; GP, Gaussian Processes; KF, Kalman Filter; LSTM, long short-term memory neural networks; MARS, multi-adaptive regression splines; ML, machine learning techniques; MLP, multi-layered perceptron; PINNs, physics-informed neural networks; PLS, partial least squares; RA, regression analysis; RF, random forests; RL, reinforcement learning; RP, random processes; SVMs, support vector machines. 10 of 30 Engineering Reports, 2025 25778196, 2025, 12, Downloaded from https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/eng2.70502, Wiley Online Library on [13/05/2026]. See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License production in a fed-batch bioreactor using Kluyveromyces marxianus, where hybrid models performed up to 12% better than the parametric models [128]. Functional Link Neural Networks (FLN) comprise 3% of the ANNs subset and have been used to predict the behavior of a series of industrial ethanol fermenters considering the effects of recycling and temperature changes [129, 130]. PINNs are an emerging implementation with 2% of the total ANN uses in hybrid modeling of bioprocesses, for example, predicting β-carotene production using Saccharomyces cerevisiae and reducing MSE by 80% when compared with a traditional parametric model [131]. Backpropagation Neural Networks (BPNN) which represent 1% of the applications have been implemented to model the production of sodium gluconate in a fermentation by Aspergillus niger, with the hybrid model yielding errors up to 87% lower than the parametric mechanistic model [132], or to identify the metabolic fluxes in a recombinant Baby Hamster Kidney culture [133]. Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) neural networks comprise 1% and have been used for predicting the pH of cream cheese fermentation at both lab and industrial scales [134] or for predicting the ethanol concentration in industrial fermenters aided by the use of Generative Adversarial Neural Networks (GAN), with 1% of the total uses of ANNs, for replacing missing datapoints of yeast [135]. In Figure 4, the Regression Analysis (RA) category refers to regression techniques that are not random processes nor ML nor ANNs, such as series expansions, polynomial expressions or time-series models like ARMAX. This category is the second biggest approach, with 16% of all the applications having a vast array of techniques at their disposal. In this category, Partial Least Squares (PLS) is the most used technique (36% of the occurrences) and has been used to describe the formation of recombinant products reducing errors by as much as three orders of magnitude when compared to traditional metabolic flux analysis [136], or to describe the mass transfer coefficient in a MBR for WTT, reducing the RMSE by 52% [137]. Linear techniques follow with 32% of the applications in this subcategory. This subcategory refers to techniques such as multivariate curve resolution coupled with mass conservation equations to describe CHO cell cultivation processes [100, 138]. Also, polynomial approaches for describing lutein production by microalgae have errors up to 5% lower than the mechanistic approach [139]. “Non-linear” refers to various techniques used in bioprocess modeling to improve predictive accuracy. One example is non-linear PLS, which has been applied to predict concentrations based on spectral data, yielding lower errors than traditional chemometric tools [140]. Another application involves modeling the specific reaction rates of a Bordetella pertussis culture by integrating both online and offline measurements, enhancing process understanding and control [112]. Similarly, multi-adaptive regression splines (MARS) have been employed in a simulated penicillin fermentation process, demonstrating superior predictive performance with mean errors 15 times lower than the parametric approach [141]. ML techniques comprise 15% of the applications. SVMs account for 47% of the applications in this subcategory and have been used in fluxomic for identifying genes to overexpress and other genes to suppress resulting in an enhanced ethanol production by 10% [142], or to optimize a nosiheptide fed-batch fermentation increasing yields by as much as 26.8% [143] or used to predict the mycelial concentration in a penicillin fermentation [144]. Reinforcement Learning (RL) accounts for 16% of the applications in this subcategory, having been used to enhance the yield prediction in a Yarrowia lipolytica fermentation process [145]. Other ML applications used are Extreme Gradient Boosting (XGB) for predicting viable cell density and glucose consumption in a CHO culture [146] or for predicting the rate constants in the reaction between alkyl esters and hydrogen atoms in biodiesel combustion processes [147]. Random Processes (RP) comprise 7.1% of the hybrid model’s applications, with examples such as GPs that account for 42% of this subcategory and have been used to predict the effluent composition of a full-scale WWT plant [148], or to predict an astaxanthin production system reducing MAPE by two orders of magnitude [149]. RF that comprise 18% of this subcategory have been used for predicting the production of a therapeutic protein in a CHO culture [146] or to predict the chemical oxygen demand and nitrate concentrations in a WTT plant with errors 8% lower than traditional activated sludge models [150]. The “Other” category accounts for 6% of the applications, mostly composed of Kalman Filter (KF) which is 32% of this subcategory and has been applied to reconstruct the experimental data from an E. coli fermentation [151], or to predict neuron activity in mammalian cells [152]. FL comprises 25% of the applications in this subcategory and has been used to predict the different stages of a fermentation thus adapting the model for each phase [153–155]. Genetic Algorithms (GA) are 25% of the approaches in this subcategory and have been adapted for optimizing hybrid models that describe E. coli fermentations [156, 157] or to identify and optimize the structure and parameters of chemical reaction networks [158]. 4.2 | Processes Where Hybrid Models Have Been Applied The type of application for which the modeling objective of the hybrid models is shown in the innermost ring of Figure 5. One hundred fifty-five different implementations were found in the literature. Most of the documents are related to Metabolite Production, processes that use hybrid models aiming to describe the concentration or behavior of a substance produced by a microorganism. The second biggest category is Cell Cultivation, where the goal of the application is to produce biomass of a microorganism. WWT is the third category, where hybrid models have been used to describe biological treatments. The “other” label accounts for processes like biosorption [159], transesterification [160], membrane separations of fermentation broths or other bio-based processes [161, 162], among others. The second ring shows how authors describe their applications. The Cell Cultivation section shows that all of them are naturally inclined to describe their processes as biomass growth. In the Metabolite Production category around half of the applications are for fermentations, followed by Protein Production and the description of Enzyme kinetics and activity of different bioprocesses, such as cellobiose using glucosidase [163], or the proteolysis of whey by alcalase [164]. In the WWT application, hybrid models have been used to describe activated sludge reactors (AS) Engineering Reports, 2025 11 of 30 25778196, 2025, 12, Downloaded from https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/eng2.70502, Wiley Online Library on [13/05/2026].
