### FIGURE 5

| Type of bioprocess hybrid models have been applied to, categorized by the main target of production: Metabolite Production, Cell cultivation for obtaining biomass, wastewater treatment (WWT), and other processes. CHO represents Chinese Hamster Ovary. AS stands for Activated Sludge reactor, and MBR stands for Membrane Bioreactor. using synthetic data generated from benchmark models [75], ANNs for predicting reaction rates in a simulated phosphorous removal plant [165]. Also, GPs are used to fill in data gaps, thus, enhancing model prediction in a parallel architecture [148], predicting the reaction rates of nitrification and denitrification in a series of tanks in an industrial sludge WWT plant [166], the predictions of pH, chemical oxygen demand and other variables in a WTT plant [115]. Besides, for WWT there are also MBRs, where the models have been used to predict biochemical oxygen demand, chemical oxygen demand, total nitrogen, and total phosphorus in a municipal WWT plant [167], suspended solids, chemical oxygen demand, and nitrite and nitrate concentration in the permeate of a MBR for WTT [109]. Also, there are applications to predict the counterion mass transfer in a membrane-supported bioreactor designed for the removal of nitrate and perchlorate from a synthetic contaminated drinking water stream [137], or to predict in real-time the phosphate concentration from municipal wastewater [168]. The outermost ring shows the products of interest most studied. The most common products for fermentation studies accounting for more than half of the applications are bio-ethanol production [135, 169–178] and penicillin [15, 61, 144, 153, 179, 180]. Other applications include the production of xanthan gum by Xanthomonas campestris [181], a fermentation using Streptomyces actuosus for producing nosiheptide [182], the production of linolenic acid [183], astaxanthin production [149], the production of sorbitol by Zymomonas mobilis [184], lactic acid production using Streptococcus [77], the production of pharmaceutics such as paclitaxel [185], and a Pseudomonas ovalis fermentation for producing gluconic acid [186]. For “Protein production,” most studies with around 57% of the documents focus on antibodies [101, 102, 187–192] and therapeutic proteins [98, 193–196]. Other proteins include streptokinase production [197], threonine production [125], and lipase production [198]. In “Cell Cultivation” applications, the field is dominated by CHO cells [122, 199–201] and Saccharomyces cerevisiae (displayed as “yeast” in Figure 5) [78, 131, 170, 202]. Other cases include the cultivation of Chlorella vulgaris [203], cultivation of Bacillus [204, 205].

black-box techniques used for developing hybrid models and the type of processes they have been applied to, where each percentage represents a fraction of the total applications. | Cross-reference of types of black box used for building hybrid models and the target processes. ANN, artificial neural networks; ML, machine learning; RA, regression analysis; RP, random processes.

### FIGURE 7

| Distribution of design patterns by type of process it has been applied to. all the different applications, with a wide margin over the different ML techniques. In Metabolite Production applications, ANNs comprise over 41% of the total applications, more than doubling all the other different black-box techniques. However, when moving to the other categories, the margin between ANNs and other types of technologies diminishes. ML techniques are the second most used black-box component overall, followed by RP, RA and other techniques. It is interesting to note that for “Metabolite Production” applications, RA is the second most used technique, but falls behind RP and ML. Other techniques are the least used, except for WWT where it becomes the second most used. One important aspect to consider is that adding the percentages of each type of application in Figure 6 does not match with the percentages given in Figure 5 due to authors exploring the use of different black-box components and combinations for their hybrid models. If a contribution uses two different black-box techniques for the same application, these are counted as separate instances (hence “double counting” some studies), which explains why “Metabolite Production” has a lower percentage and the other categories have higher percentages when compared to Figure 5. The most common architecture used is the “feature-learning” design pattern with 83% of the applications, followed by “delta model” implementations and “physics-based preprocessing” with 15% and 2% respectively (Figure 7). Some authors use a mixture of both parallel and serial architectures [144, 148, 206]. In all the design patterns, metabolite production comprises the Engineering Reports, 2025 13 of 30 25778196, 2025, 12, Downloaded from https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/eng2.70502, Wiley Online Library on [13/05/2026]. See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License bulk of the applications, although inside the “delta model” implementations, the margin is thinner than in “feature learning” applications. It is important to note that Cell Cultivation processes have the same share in “feature learning” implementations than “delta model”. However, this is not the case for WWT, signaling that authors prefer “delta model” implementations when dealing with these case studies. This might be due to higher uncertainty and less mechanistic knowledge available when modeling the complex processes of WWT. Many of the WWT applications use hybrid models to predict contaminant concentrations [75, 148] or physicochemical parameters like BOD, pH, and COD [207, 208]. Regarding the outputs of the nonparametric components of hybrid models, common predictions are kinetic or transport parameters with 55% of the contributions, followed by 19% that predict reaction rates, 11% directly predict concentrations, and 15% predict other quantities such as pH, fluxes, or volumetric flows. “Delta model” architectures usually model the residuals or the mismatch [209–211].

### Figure 8 shows the distribution of the microorganisms and

enzymes used in processes where hybrid models were implemented (no matter if the goal was cell cultivation or metabolite production). Saccharomyces cerevisiae is the most studied microorganism with the largest number of applications coming from ethanol production or cell cultivation [67, 78, 131, 135, 170, 173, 202, 212–216]. Then, E. coli which has been mainly used for antibody and protein production [99, 151, 194, 217–220], has also been used for producing antivenom [221]. CHO has been mainly used in therapeutic production processes [51, 100, 122, 146, 199–201], with the three accounting for more than half of all applications. Pichia pastoris has been studied mainly for protein production [188, 195, 222], whereas Zymomonas mobilis has been used to describe sorbitol production [184] and ethanol production [174, 175]. The label “Bacteria” refers to unspecified bacteria, usually associated with mixed cultures in WWT [223, 224], but also Enterobacter A47 to produce exopolysaccharides [225], or the production of bacterial cellobiose by Acetobacter xylinum [226]. The category “Other” encompasses 32 different microorganisms and includes MA-104 (epithelial cells from the kidney of an African green monkey) for vaccine development [227], Kluyveromyces lactis for producing galactooligosaccharides [123], Xanthophyllomyces dendrorhous for producing astaxanthin [149], mixed microbial cultures for producing polyhydroxyalkanoates [228], Yarrowia lipolytica for citrate production [145], Cunninghamella echinulata for linolenic acid production [183], Pseudomonas putida used in the production of polyhydroxyalkanoates [229], Aspergillus niger in a sodium gluconate fermentation [132], Ralstonia eutropha in the production of poly-β-hydroxybutyrate [230], Candida rugosa in the production of lipase [198] among others. 4.3 | Type of Data and Mode of Operation Most of the hybrid models’ applications use experimental data for their development (80% of the total). The remaining 20% of the contributions use simulated data, usually derived from other mechanistic models such as the production of gluconic acid by Pseudomonas ovalis [231], describing the interactions between

### FIGURE 8

| Most used microorganisms in the works for developing hybrid models. Chlorella vulgaris and Stutzerimonas stutzeri for chlorophyll accumulation [232], applying inverse PINNs to model complex reaction networks [74], E. coli for recombinant protein production [233], adapting symbolic regression to infer the structure and parameters when building hybrid models [234] or unnamed biological systems [235, 236]. It is important to note that hybrid models trained on simulated data can present a high level of bias because of the propagation error of the models used for producing the data.

### Figure 9 shows the scale of the experiments and the operation mode used to generate the calibration data. On the left,

the scale of the experimental data used for developing hybrid models is compared to the type of application (as shown in the inner ring of Figure 5). The most common scale is the laboratory for all applications. For pilot scale, cell cultivation applications include BHK-21 [133] and an undisclosed microorganism [79], Metabolite production applications at the pilot scale include cellulose production using Acetobacter xylinus [226], optimization and control of a Bacillus thuringiensis fermentation [237], a methyl methacrylate polymerization reactor, the production of a foreign protein by an undisclosed bacteria [223], several studies using Pichia pastoris for antibody production [188, 222, 238], Aspergillus niger fermentations for glycerol production [119] and sodium gluconate [132], using Streptomyces actuosus for nosiheptide production [182], diverse cases of E. coli for protein production [192, 219], ethanol production using S. cerevisiae [64, 114], and therapeutic protein production using CHO [100]. Pilot scale WWT data have been used to build soft sensors for modeling the phosphorous removal in a biofilm reactor [116], prediction of effluent quality in an activated sludge reactor, and control of critical parameters in an alternating aerobic-anoxic WWT plant [166]. At industrial scales, there are several applications for cell cultivation of S. cerevisiae [78, 239] and a CHO cultivation process [240]. ### FIGURE 9

| Left: Number of studies showing the scale of the experimental data used for developing the hybrid models according to the type of application. Right: Relationships between the scale of the data used for developing hybrid models and the mode of operation. an undisclosed industrial fermentation modeled using deep neural networks [126], pH prediction at an industrial cream cheese fermentation [134], erythromycin fermentation [242], and E. coli for therapeutic protein production [59, 99]. Other applications at industrial scale include ultrafiltration of fermentation broth [162], chromatographic processes for protein capture [243], and WWT [66, 109, 115, 148]. In Figure 9 on the right, the cross-reference of scale and mode of operation is shown. Batch experiments are the most common accounting for 44.6% of the category; fed batch and continuous systems trail behind with 33.7% and 20.7% of the applications respectively, while around 1% of the contributions do not report the scale of their experiments. Industrial datasets comprise 21.7% of the contributions, with pilot scale datasets accounting for 25.3% and laboratory scale including 53% of the contributions. Industrial batch applications account for 9.7% of the contributions, with continuous systems accounting for 9.6% and fed batch systems including 2.4% of the contributions. At the pilot scale, batch reactors comprise 12% of the contributions, with continuous and fed-batch reactors accounting for 3.6% and 9.6% respectively. Finally, at the lab scale, continuous reactors comprise 8.4% of the contributions, with fed-batch accounting for 21.7% and batch representing 22.9% of the contributions. 4.4 | Computational Aspects Only around 40.3% of the documents report on the software used for implementing the hybrid models. Figure 10 at the left shows the distribution of software, whereas on the right a cross-reference of the software and the nonparametric structures is shown as described in Figure 4. ANNs are the preferred nonparametric structure used in every software, followed by Regression techniques. Genetic algorithms were found only in MATLAB applications whereas Python presents several ML techniques, primarily developed in TensorFlow, PyTorch and Sci-kit learn packages. Most of the hybrid models have been implemented in MATLAB [17, 59, 99, 164, 244–246], with Python rising in recent years [122, 147, 209, 220]. Some applications have been developed in software like C# [241], Julia [131], Statistica [163, 181], or FORTRAN [74, 247].

### Figure 11 shows the performance indicators for evaluating hybrid

model’s fit. The most used performance indicator is the family of the MSE, which encompasses 45% of the occurrences (244 performance indicators used to evaluate models). Authors have used MSE in various forms, such as ROOT MSE (RMSE), normalized MSE (NMSE), RMSE of predicted values (RMSEP), weighted MSE (WMSE), and base MSE among others. The second most used indicator is r2 (Coefficient of determination), appearing in 25% of the applications, even though it may not be suitable for many non-linear problems. The Information Criteria (IC) such as Akaike Information Criterion (AIC) or Bayesian Information Criterion (BIC) are very useful for determining the structure of the nonparametric sub model [11, 57]. Errors such as MAPE, MAE, mean relative error (MRE), mean average error (MAER), residual standard error (RSE), and mean relative percentage error (MRPE) make up almost 11% of the instances. The “Other” category is dominated by sum of squared errors (SSE) and residual standard deviation (RSD). 4.5 | Recent Trends Recent applications of hybrid modeling include the work of [248], where the authors use Bayesian inference applied to a Neural Network designed to predict both the reaction rates and stoichiometry from simulated data. AIC, Akaike Information Criterion; BIC, Bayesian Information Criterion; IC, Information Criteria; MAE, mean absolute error; MAER, mean average error; MAPE, mean absolute percentage error; MRE, mean relative error; MRPE, Mean relative percentage error; MSE, mean squared error; NMSE, normalized MSE; RMSE, root MSE; RMSEP, of predicted values; RSD, residual standard deviation; RSE, residual standard error; SSE, sum of squared errors; WMSE, weighted MSE. pure data-driven technique, obtaining narrower confidence intervals and better prediction capabilities. The production of threonine applying Deep Learning techniques for the nonparametric model, obtains low errors when compared to the synthetic ANN training dataset [125]. Deep Learning was also applied to model a 50 L Pichia pastoris cultivation, increasing prediction accuracy by 18.4% and reducing CPU time by 43.4% when compared to a shallow ANN [238]. Hybrid models have proven to perform better 16 of 30 Engineering Reports, 2025 25778196, 2025, 12, Downloaded from https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/eng2.70502, Wiley Online Library on [13/05/2026]. See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License for antibody production than traditional parametric models [24, 102, 249]. A hybrid model was developed using data from 300 mL flasks and applied to describe the process in a 15 L bioreactor, with the error being in the same range as the analytical error and that of a traditional regression model [100]. A 100 kilogallons fermenter has been recently studied [126]. The authors carry out a sensitivity analysis of the traditional kinetic model to better understand which factors affect the output the most. The identified kinetic parameters are selected as candidates to be modeled using a Deep Neural Network. Of the candidates, a clustering analysis is done to identify those that vary with time, and the ones that have time dependence were selected as the parameters to be modeled by the nonparametric structure. The resultant hybrid model outperforms the traditional kinetic model, reducing the RMSE by a factor of 10. Some authors have used hybrid models to infer the structure of the mechanistic model [105]. A hybrid model using RL to regress the kinetic parameters for two proposed parametric structures was developed. The used framework correctly identified that a Monod-like kinetic structure was wrong because the parameter values were 0. In the same study, the authors trained a hybrid model on synthetic data (generated using the identified models) which predicted time-variant kinetic parameters, achieving a MAPE lower than 5% for an experimental batch run. Finally, they investigated the effect of noise in the hybrid model’s prediction. The MAPE deteriorated to 17% when 30% noise was added, indicating that the proposed framework can deal appropriately with noisy data. β-carotene production using Saccharomyces cerevisiae has been studied [131]. The authors used a Universal Differential Equation (UDE) to develop their hybrid model. In the UDE approach, a neural ordinary differential (an ordinary differential equation that is described by a neural network) equation that approximates the derivatives of the previously unknown dynamics of the batch fermentation process is integrated with the ordinary differential equations (ODEs) of its kinetic model to give a hybrid model with superior accuracy. The UDE model is compared with a traditional kinetic model, resulting in better predictions and lower MSE. Their work shows that neural ODEs can be integrated into hybrid models to describe kinetic parameters and their changes over time. The level of “grayness” in hybrid models was investigated by [183] in a Cunninghamella echinulata fermentation. Experiments were carried out at a 1 L fermentation and the models were tested in a 5 L bioreactor. Three hybrid models were developed, using GPs as a nonparametric structure, with an increasing degree of mechanistic knowledge in the kinetic expression. The model with the most mechanistic knowledge performed worse, indicating a high risk of inducing bias that hinders the model’s performance. The hybrid models showed similar performance in the 1 and the 5 L training datasets. The model with intermediate mechanistic knowledge performed better, with lower uncertainty and error. Introducing limited mechanistic knowledge resulted in overfitting issues. Their results show that incorporating kinetic knowledge that is not well understood can harm a hybrid model’s performance. A similar study was conducted in a CHO bioreactor [122]. Authors investigated several parametric and nonparametric techniques. However, they also investigated the “grayness” of hybrid models. Different hybrid models replacing one flux and up to five kinetic parameters predicted by the nonparametric technique were tested against an established parametric model. The hybrid models reproduced the design space, although on some edges the predictions were flatter than the ones given by the parametric model. Digital shadows, which are in silico representations of physical systems that capture and aggregate data from their real-world counterparts and have been built as a decision-making tool, present themselves as an emerging use case for hybrid models [250]. Recently, authors developed a digital shadow by coupling a mechanistic model with a purely data-driven soft sensor for estimating glucose and glycerol concentrations [119]. They studied a pilot (volumes of 550 and 2500 L) Aspergillus niger fermentation. The model was trained in 55 batch experiments with 4 additional calibration runs. To study scalability, 8 tests at the lab scale were performed. For the development of the data-driven soft sensor, a variety of models were tested, including ANNs, SVM, RF, Partial Least Squares, XGB, and LGBM. Their implementation achieved a normalized residual MSE of 2% with an r2 of 0.98. The model predicted biomass, glucose, and glycerol concentrations accurately for the three scales, indicating that hybrid models could be developed in smaller scales and later implemented in bigger scales, and still retain high prediction accuracy. Hybrid models have been recently used to accelerate the design and optimization of monoclonal antibody production [191]. The methodology used in silico experimental runs with a model derived by other authors [251]. The in silico experiments were used to train a hybrid model that follows a “feature learning” design pattern that uses an ANN as a nonparametric component. After the hybrid model training, a genetic algorithm was used to optimize the feeding schedule that maximizes the antibody titer at harvest, and after identifying the optimal conditions, another in silico experiment was run to assess the reliability of the predicted values. This strategy was compared to a traditional response surface methodology optimization strategy. The hybrid model correctly identified the optimal feeding strategy within 1% of the real strategy and showed a 34.9% increase in antibody titer when compared to training data and a 2.8% higher concentration than the optimal results from traditional response surface methodology. However, the predicted titer of the hybrid model was 18.6% lower than the real one, because the authors were extrapolating the hybrid model outside of its training region. Adding one more in silico experiment that covered the extrapolation region reduced the prediction error to 8.7%. LSTMs have been recently applied to model a downstream separation in an industrial biorefinery manufacturing 1,4-bio-butanediol [162]. The authors utilized a PLS model for estimating trans-membrane pressure, which was then coupled with a mechanistic knowledge model that follows Darcy’s equation, in a “feature learning” design pattern. The mechanistic knowledge model estimated the membrane resistances, and the hybrid model was deployed to predict short-term and long-term fouling of membranes, achieving r2 higher than 0.9. The authors claim that the model can identify irreversible and reversible Engineering Reports, 2025 17 of 30 25778196, 2025, 12, Downloaded from https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/eng2.70502, Wiley Online Library on [13/05/2026]. See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License fouling, allowing for better analysis of performance and more in-depth diagnostics. Hybrid models have been used in genome-scale metabolic modeling to optimize ethanol production using S. cerevisiae [142]. Authors propose a hybrid model-driven platform that integrates flux balance analysis and several ML techniques, such as SVM, MLPs, RFs, and several linear regression techniques. The study highlights the importance of combining metabolic modeling and ML to unravel the complex relationships between genotype, phenotype, and the environment. The hybrid model identified key genetic modifications, including the overexpression of six target genes and the knockout of seven target genes, which significantly improved ethanol yield. Experiments showed that knocking out succinate dehydrogenase genes increased ethanol production by 6%–10% compared to the wild-type strain. Further dual-gene deletion experiments targeting glycerol-3-phosphate dehydrogenase and succinate dehydrogenase resulted in even higher ethanol yields, with improvements of up to 27.9%. Recently, dynamic modeling encompassing first principles and recurrent neural networks (RNN-LSTM) has been developed to enhance the sporulation process of Bacillus licheniformis BF-002 [204]. This innovative approach aimed to improve spore production by optimizing cultivation conditions, including carbon/nitrogen source feeding rates, pH, temperature, and agitation speed. The researchers cultivated the strain in a 5 L fermenter. The hybrid model was implemented in a “feature learning” design pattern: a traditional dynamic model that described the temporal variations of vegetative cell quantity and nutrient concentrations during growth and sporulation, and an RNN-LSTM model that predicted kinetic parameters dynamically influenced by environmental conditions. The hybrid model achieved a reduction of MRE of up to 82% in the number of vegetative cell quantities and 22% in the spore quantities when compared to a parametric model. The hybrid model was used with genetic algorithms to find the optimal feeding conditions that maximize spore production. This approach resulted in a 35.21% increase in sporulation, highlighting its effectiveness in optimizing cultivation dynamics. Transfer learning approaches have been recently implemented to take advantage of existing knowledge of one CHO process to another [146]. Authors used MLPs, RFs, XGBoost to predict kinetic parameters in a “feature learning” design pattern. There were two sets of experiments that produced different antibodies. One set was used for training and calibrating the hybrid models (dataset A) and the other was used for testing the transfer learning capabilities (dataset B). To correctly identify the adequate inputs to the nonparametric techniques, a series of correlation analyses and multi-collinearity were performed, to minimize the number of inputs that need to be measured. The three hybrid models perform similarly in the test experiments in dataset A, with an NRMSE of 0.07. In dataset B, predictions had slightly higher NRMSE (less than 0.02), with RFs performing the best. Authors also investigated performance when training the hybrid models in dataset B; however, they observed that the cumulative amount of glucose predictions did not improve, contrary to viable cell density and titer. Neural ODEs are used as expansion for non-accounted dynamics in the structure of parametric models [214]. The authors carried out experimental fed-batch cultivations using an engineered β-carotene producing S. cerevisiae strain. Over 50 parametric model structures were tested, identifying the best one. After identifying the best parametric structure, the Neural ODEs were implemented as an additive term to the ODEs describing biomass growth and ethanol production. The hybrid model exhibited 3.6-fold and 1.7-fold better performance in the training and testing datasets, when compared to the best parametric model structure. Despite longer training and calibration times than the parametric model, the hybrid model executed faster. There is a growing interest in applying hybrid modeling for commercial and industrial uses, with AspenTech filing for a patent [252, 253], the foundation of companies specialized in the area like DataHow AG and Novasign, and a recent interest on behalf of the pharmaceutical industry [59, 99, 101, 102]. 4.6 | Synthesis of Key Findings Hybrid models in bioprocesses predominantly employ ANNs as the data-driven component, accounting for 58% of publications. ANNs have been widely applied across various bioprocesses, from cell cultivation to pharmaceutical production. Other ML techniques, such as SVMs, have also been utilized, particularly in cell cultivation and WTT. In lesser proportion, parametric techniques such as polynomials and sigmoidal expressions have been applied successfully to metabolic fluxes analysis [136], and lutein production [139]. From an application perspective, various categories of data-driven techniques have been successfully employed in bioprocessing. However, there is no consensus on the most suitable method for each process type, despite a clear preference for ANNs. Over 63% of work targets metabolite production—chiefly fermentation and protein synthesis—while ̃40% address pharmaceutical operations (penicillin, therapeutic proteins, antibodies, CHO cells) [101, 102]. As such, the enhanced prediction capabilities of hybrid models combined with specific approaches to experimental design [59, 98, 99] have led to innovation in the field. WTT comprises ̃10% of cases but leads in industrial uptake, underscoring hybrid models’ aptitude for revealing hidden dynamics under sparse monitoring. In terms of hybrid model architecture, feature learning hybrids dominate despite offering no statistically significant performance advantage over delta or serial patterns. Their popularity likely stems from built-in interpretability, conservation law enforcement, and modular training, which suit the multi-step, partially observed nature of bioprocesses [20]. Ethanol fermentations of S. cerevisiae remain the most intensely studied system—driven by abundant data—while recent work has diversified to include E. coli, P. pastoris, CHO cells (collectively 30%), and a broad “other” category (32%). Studies equally showcase hybrid models across batch (44.6%), fed batch (33.7%) and continuous (20.7%) modes of operation and at lab (53%), pilot (25%) and industrial (21.7%) scales, underscoring their adaptability [119]. 18 of 30 Engineering Reports, 2025 25778196, 2025, 12, Downloaded from https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/eng2.70502, Wiley Online Library on [13/05/2026]. See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License Software choice and model evaluation are areas of persistent inconsistency. MATLAB remains the predominant simulation environment, although Python is gaining traction. Model performance assessment still is variant: about a quarter of studies still rely on r2—despite its failure to penalize over parameterization and insensitivity to nonlinear dynamics [94, 254]. On the other hand, 45% of contributions use MSE derivatives to calculate their performance indexes, despite it being highly sensitive to outliers [255]. This last selection might be since it is a default performance index in ML toolboxes. To foster more robust and interpretable assessments, we foresee that these performance indexes could be complemented with information-theoretic and distribution-based measures (e.g., entropy coefficients, Kolmogorov–Smirnov statistics, Log-Cosh and quantile losses) and adopting principled model-comparison criteria such as Watanabe Akaike Information Criterion (WAIC) or Leave-One-Out Cross-Validation Information Criterion (LOO-CVI) [256–259]. Despite three decades of development, hybrid bioprocess modeling still lacks standardized protocols for selecting design patterns, balancing mechanistic and data-driven components, quantifying uncertainty and defining data preprocessing workflows. The field’s reliance on trial and error and experience—evidenced by 374 individual model implementations versus 155 distinct case studies—underscores this gap. Some efforts have been made recently to highlight practical and technical details of implementing hybrid models, such as reviews that present brief guides for developing them [25, 260], but more work is needed. Due to having both a parametric/mechanistic component and a data-driven/nonparametric component, assessing how much uncertainty comes from each remains an unexplored avenue. The field could benefit from recent developments in the ML field, where authors suggest a Monte Carlo approach to generate testing datasets and better capture uncertainty [261]. The absence of standardized guidelines in hybrid bioprocess modeling arises precisely from its defining strength: structural flexibility. Hybrid frameworks can accommodate a wide range of design choices—yet still outperform purely mechanistic models—while demanding far less data than fully nonparametric approaches and preserving a degree of interpretability that renders them practical for industrial deployment. However, this very hybrid character also complicates evaluation: conventional metrics and workflows developed for parametric or nonparametric models often fail to capture the unique tradeoffs in uncertainty quantification and mechanistic parameter estimation inherent to hybrid systems. Numerical workflows in hybrid bioprocess modeling are similarly under specified. Critical steps—data pretreatment methods (both numerical and knowledge based), augmentation strategies, and the rationale for selecting inputs to the data driven module—are frequently omitted, leaving reproducibility and comparability in doubt. Moreover, cross-validation protocols and data partitioning schemes are rarely described, despite their importance for robust training and unbiased assessment of models that rely heavily on high-quality data to capture complex, industrially relevant dynamics. To address these gaps, the community should adopt rigorous information-theoretic tools to quantify intervariable relationships and guide model structure, and standardize transparent cross-validation procedures to ensure reliable, reproducible hybrid model development [262]. Cross validation offers clear advantages—correcting selection bias, quantifying estimation uncertainty (particularly for nonparametric components) and guiding model complexity to prevent overfitting [263]. Yet, despite its value, consensus on hyperparameter tuning remains elusive. While many practitioners adhere to the conventional three-way split (training, validation, test) coupled with grid, random or Bayesian searches, limited data often forces a two-set approach (training and validation only). Without systematic cross validation, hybrid models inherit latent selection bias, further obscuring true predictive uncertainty. Although a handful of specialized diagnostic tools exist for assessing hybrid performance, the community still lacks standardized protocols for data partitioning and hyperparameter optimization—an important frontier for improving both transparency and reliability in hybrid bioprocess modeling. The challenges outlined above often originate in insufficient data quantity and quality, underscoring the necessity of integrating hybrid-model data requirements into the experimental design phase. In this context, Intensified Design of Experiments (iDoE) offers a compelling solution: by strategically varying inputs and conditions, iDoE minimizes the total number of trials while maximizing the informational content of each run, thereby furnishing richer datasets that better support the calibration and validation of hybrid bioprocess models [59, 98, 99, 117]. Of course, this strategy is also limited by monitoring capabilities, since online data acquisition of primary variables at high frequency still is a challenge. Developments in process analytical technologies (PAT) definitely will catalyze the advanced modeling strategies as hybrid models. 4.7 | Hybrid Modeling Challenges Certain challenges for implementing hybrid models have been mentioned in the literature [26, 55, 264]:

## 1. The mechanistic part of the hybrid model constrains the

space of solutions obtainable, which helps avoid pitfalls such as negative concentrations. These constraints impose an inductive bias into the responses, which might negatively affect the hybrid model’s performance if the mechanistic part is not suitable for describing the process.

## 2. There are standard techniques for identifying parameters

in statistical or purely parametric models. For hybrid models, only a handful of parameter identification methods have been reported [57, 95, 104, 265]. The major hurdle is the time needed for parameter identification, due to the extensive calculations that should be performed.

## 3. In addressing the initial challenge, the integration of hybrid

models requires a thorough examination of the foundational assumptions inherent in the mechanistic knowledge to be integrated. This task can be approached via two distinct methodologies: Engineering Reports, 2025 19 of 30 25778196, 2025, 12, Downloaded from https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/eng2.70502, Wiley Online Library on [13/05/2026]. See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License a. The incremental approach, where the most basic assumptions are made, and more complex interactions are added until the model performance decreases. b. The removal approach, where the most complex fundamental structure is evaluated first, and then features are removed iteratively to determine what part is hindering the model’s performance. Both approaches are time consuming, so model development will require some experience or an analysis of a risk of mechanistic knowledge being incorrect.

## 4. Validating and verifying a hybrid model is another challenge, because there needs to be a balance of complexity,

accuracy, interpretability and scalability which is hard to optimize. Moreover, in hybrid models, nonparametric and parametric components can introduce distinct sources of uncertainty and error that must be carefully managed [26]. 5. The level of “grayness” in hybrid models—the balance between mechanistic knowledge and data-driven components—can significantly impact performance. As illustrated by Rogers et al. [183], incorporating too much or too little mechanistic knowledge can lead to bias or overfitting, respectively, thus hindering model performance. This underscores the need for meticulous calibration and validation of hybrid models to ensure optimal balance and performance. Regardless of advancements in key areas, still there is not a unified framework for determining the valid region for extrapolation of hybrid models. Besides the challenges addressed before, we propose the following ones that emerge from the literature review:

## 1. There

are no consumer-ready applications built on open-source software for building hybrid models easily, which hinders its adoption by industry, due to the need for specialized personnel to build, operate and maintain the models. Although some custom toolboxes for MATLAB like HYBMOD [125, 220, 222, 233] exist, source code is poorly documented, and a licensing agreement should be signed.

## 2. As with many pure data-driven techniques, there is not

a consolidated framework that allows to select the proper nonparametric technique a priori, meaning that a costly process of trial and error is necessary to build hybrid models.

## 3. Like the previous challenge, there is a lack of a general and

unified framework for deciding which type of hybrid model structure/design pattern is best suited for an application, which also results in a resource-intensive search.

## 4. As with many nonparametric techniques, how data should

be split between training, testing and validations has no consensus. Authors commonly only use training and validation splits due to constraints on the availability of high-quality data, while few report using datasets for the three purposes. 5. There is no consensus on the training hierarchy of the parametric and nonparametric components of hybrid models. When using the “delta model” design pattern, the training of each subcomponent is done separately. However, when using the other design patterns, there are no clear guidelines on how to address the identification of the parametric components and the nonparametric components of hybrid models. Some authors choose the best pure parametric model structure available, start hybridizing and then calibrating the nonparametric structure over it [214]. Other authors do the calibration of both components at the same time [266, 267]. Whether one approach yields better results than the other needs to be further investigated. 4.8 | Hybrid Modeling Perspectives Looking forward, several promising avenues for future research and development in hybrid semi-parametric modeling are clear. One key area is the continued refinement of model integration techniques. Advancements and applications of deep learning [125, 220, 222] and the development of sophisticated neural network architectures offer opportunities to enhance the nonparametric components of hybrid models. The application of Bayesian inference, could improve the accuracy and confidence of predictions by incorporating uncertainty quantification into the modeling process [248]. Additionally, expanding the application of hybrid models to new bioprocesses and products will be crucial. As the bioprocessing industry continues to innovate and develop new products, hybrid models will need to adapt and evolve to meet these emerging challenges. The growing interest from commercial and industrial sectors, including companies like AspenTech and DataHow AG, as well as the pharmaceutical industry, further underscores the relevance and applicability of hybrid models in real-world settings. The development of standardized frameworks and best practices for hybrid model implementation could facilitate broader adoption and integration into industrial processes. Authors should remain open-minded and explore solutions and implementations beyond hybrid modeling in bioprocesses or applied mathematics or computer science; for instance, disciplines like agriculture and meteorology have yielded promising hybrid modeling strategies [268–270]. Although implementations are dependent on data quality and specific to each field, general insights and strategies can be adapted. Hybrid semi-parametric models represent a powerful tool for advancing bioprocess modeling and optimization. Integrating mechanistic and data-driven approaches offers a pathway to more accurate, reliable, and scalable models that can meet the complex demands of modern bioprocessing. Continued research and innovation in this field will undoubtedly unlock new opportunities and enhance the efficiency and effectiveness of bioprocesses across various industries. 5 | Concluding Remarks This work presents a comprehensive framework for hybrid modeling in bioprocesses, drawing insights from 270 references. It covers hybrid models’ characteristics, architecture, and nonparametric structures. Hybrid models are primarily 20 of 30 Engineering Reports, 2025 25778196, 2025, 12, Downloaded from https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/eng2.70502, Wiley Online Library on [13/05/2026]. See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License applied to metabolite production, especially ethanol and penicillin fermentations—followed by cell cultivation and WTT. In protein production, antibody manufacturing is significant. ANNs are the dominant nonparametric method, though other ML techniques such as random processes and genetic algorithms are also used. Most models are trained on experimental data from laboratory-scale studies, with some full-scale industrial applications. Batch and fed-batch systems are the most common operation modes, while continuous processes are less explored. Frequently used microorganisms include Saccharomyces cerevisiae, E. coli, and CHO cells. MATLAB is the preferred software, though Python is gaining popularity due to its robust ML toolboxes. Finally, while MSE and related metrics are common, no consensus exists on how to best evaluate hybrid model performance. PSE provides the critical link between these hybrid-model capabilities and global sustainability imperatives. By embedding life-cycle assessment, techno-economic analysis and multi-objective optimization within hybrid modeling workflows, PSE enables quantitative trade-off studies that reconcile yield, energy consumption, emissions and cost from early design through scale-up. Digital-twin simulations and real-time soft sensors further ensure adaptive control and resilience under variable feedstocks. Such integrated PSE-driven approaches directly support UN SDG 7 (clean energy), SDG 9 (industry and innovation), SDG 12 (responsible production) and SDG 13 (climate action) by valorizing biomass waste, minimizing environmental impact and fostering low-carbon biomanufacturing. Standardizing PSE-embedded sustainability metrics within hybrid models will be pivotal for accelerating tangible SDG progress and closing the financing and implementation gaps that currently jeopardize the 2030 agenda. # Fermentation design and process optimization strategy based on machine learning

## LLM-ready full-text Markdown

This Markdown file was generated from a PDF. Images were omitted. Extracted figure captions, table text, equations, references, and article body text are retained where the PDF text layer exposed them. The layout was converted into a single reading order for LLM/RAG ingestion.

## Minimal metadata

Review Article Fermentation design and process optimization strategy based on machine learning Zhen-Zhi Wang a, Du-Wen Zeng a, Yi-Fan Zhu a, Ming-Hai Zhou a, Akihiko Kondo b, Tomohisa Hasunuma b, Xin-Qing Zhao a,* a State Key Laboratory of Microbial Metabolism, and School of Life Sciences & Biotechnology, Shanghai Jiao Tong University, Shanghai, 200240, China b Engineering Biology Research Center, Kobe University, 1-1 Rokkodai, Nada, Kobe, 657-8501, Japan A R T I C L E I N F O Keywords: Machine learning Fermentation optimization Automated fermentation process control Efﬁcient bioproduction Process Design

## ABSTRACT

Fermentation optimization is important for industrialization of biological manufacturing, and has been widely applied to diverse sectors including medicine, food, cosmetics and bioenergy, which is related to substantial economic beneﬁts. Strain development is considered to be the core part of fermentation technology, as it directly inﬂuences the product yield and overall success of the fermentation process. However, fermentation design and process optimization also play a crucial role in fully exploring the genetic potential of engineered strains for efﬁcient bioproduction. Due to the fact that fermentation process is inﬂuenced by complex factors, so far, machine learning has been widely used in this area with its strong capabilities of simulation and prediction. This review provides a brief introduction to the process of fermentation design and process optimization based on machine learning. In the workﬂow, experimental design strategy is fundamental to explore and characterize the performance of fermentation system. Then, machine learning modelling is employed to simulate the operation of fermentation system and the appropriate fermentation conditions, such as medium composition and process parameters, will be determined. Moreover, in recent years, some extension ideas of fermentation design based on machine learning have also been proposed, including automated fermentation process control, data mining for exploring strain characteristics, transfer learning, hybrid model building, and soft sensor construction. These strategies have created more application scenarios for machine learning, enhancing its adaptability in designing and optimizing the complex fermentation system for efﬁcient bioproduction.

## 1. Introduction

Fermentation is the process by which speciﬁc metabolic pathways of microorganisms are utilized to produce metabolites. With the rapid development of industry, modern fermentation technology enables largescale production of bio-based products. Using renewable substances as raw materials, fermentation technology contributes to sustainable economic growth, which is beneﬁcial to carbon peaking and carbon neutrality goals. Strain development is the key issue of fermentation engineering, but it is also crucial to design and optimize fermentation process from the perspective of systematic optimization to achieve efﬁcient bioproduction. In the fermentation process, temperature [1,2], pH [3,4], dissolved oxygen [5], nutritional conditions (nitrogen source [6], carbon source [7], mineral salts and vitamins [8]), feeding strategy [9] and other factors will strongly impact the titer and yield of ﬁnal products (Table 1), which should be optimized in order to fully exploit the production capacity of engineered strains. Due to the complex and dynamic interaction between microorganisms and fermentation environment, applying mechanism models to reﬂect speciﬁc effects of various fermentation conditions and simulate operation of fermentation system poses a great challenge [10]. For example, fermentation kinetics models are mathematical models describing cell growth, substrate utilization and product generation [11]. However, these models often struggle to fully represent the complex internal metabolic processes within organisms and fail to predict the impact of factors beyond substrate concentration. Therefore, empirical models are often utilized to represent the input-output relationships between various factors and fermentation results. Empirical models are black-box models that do not consider the internal mechanisms, and methods of machine learning (ML), a subset of artiﬁcial intelligence (AI), are commonly used to construct such models [12]. ML refers to using empirical data and speciﬁc algorithms on computer * Corresponding author. E-mail address: xqzhao@sjtu.edu.cn (X.-Q. Zhao). Contents lists available at ScienceDirect BioDesign Research journal homepage: www.sciencedirect.com/journal/biodesign-research https://doi.org/10.1016/j.bidere.2025.100002 Received 1 November 2024; Received in revised form 31 December 2024; Accepted 16 January 2025 Available online 26 February 2025 2693-1257/© 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of Nanjing Agricultural University. This is an open access article under the CC BY license (http:// creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/). BioDesign Research 7 (2025) 100002 to generate models with predictive functions [13]. This process is data-driven, which derives statistical relationships among variables from training data. Random Forests (RF) [14], Support Vector Machine (SVM) [15] and Artiﬁcial Neural Network (ANN) [16] are typical ML algorithms for simulating and optimizing the fermentation process. By capturing the input-output relationship of fermentation system, these models are able to uncover the complex patterns of interaction and identify optimal fermentation conditions. In addition, ML methods hold great potential for modifying metabolic pathways in strains, which can identify potential overexpression targets for strain engineering [17], screen for the best combination of biological elements [18], guide the ﬁne tuning of enzyme levels [19], facilitate de novo promoter design [20], and predict the impact of 5’ untranslated region on translation efﬁciency [21]. Today, advancements in synthetic biology have accelerated the speed of strain development and iteration, imposing higher demands on the efﬁciency of fermentation optimization for newly engineered strains. In the era of AI, high-performance ML algorithms and high-throughput fermentation test equipment have been developed, providing impetus for rapid development in the ﬁeld of fermentation optimization [22]. Some existing reviews have summarized the progress of ML-based fermentation optimization [23–27]. However, a comprehensive introduction to the overall processes and methodologies involved is still lacking, and there is no centralized categorization of the extended applications of ML methods in this ﬁeld. To meet these challenges, here we summarize the algorithms and the concrete workﬂow of ML-based fermentation design and optimization (Fig. 1), comprising experimental design strategy for fermentation test, ML modeling methods, model evaluation, hyperparameter tuning and prediction as well as extension ideas of ML-based fermentation design.

## 2. Experimental design for fermentation test

Fermentation test serves as a cornerstone for investigating and characterizing the performance of fermentation system. So far, Onefactor-at-a-time (OFAT) experiments such as removal experiments and supplementation experiments have been widely used [27], despite being time-consuming and yielding unstable results. To enhance the effectiveness of fermentation test, a comprehensive process development strategy has been developed, like DOE (Design of Experiment) strategy based on statistical principles [28], as depicted in Fig. 2, including Factorial Design [29], Plackett-Burman Design [30], Orthogonal Design [31], Uniform Design [32], Central Composite Design [33] and Box-Behnken Design [34]. The task of DOE can be facilitated by relevant software, such as Design Expert - a software tailored for experimental design developed by Stat-Ease, which can generate various experimental design schemes and conduct basic statistical analysis [35].

### Table 1

Key factors in fermentation process. Factor Mechanism Example Temperature Affecting cell growth [1,2] Changing enzyme activity Affecting physical properties of fermentation broth pH Causing physiological stress [3,4] Altering metabolic characteristics Triggering chemical reactions Stirring speed and ventilation Changing dissolved oxygen levels [5] Improving the overall homogeneity to promote the cell's absorption of dissolved oxygen and nutrients Nitrogen source Participating in protein synthesis [6] Changing osmotic pressure and pH Carbon source Providing matter and energy for metabolism [7] Changing osmotic pressure and pH Mineral salts and vitamins Supporting life activities as important cofactors [8] Feeding rate Ensuring appropriate substrate supply [9] Avoiding metabolic adjustments

### Fig. 1. The workﬂow of ML-based fermentation

design and process optimization. Strain development is the ﬁrst step of fermentation engineering. Then, fermentation test is applied to explore and characterize the performance of fermentation system. By ML modelling and prediction, the appropriate fermentation parameters will be determined. The results of fermentation optimization can be used as reference and basis for strain development. Before industrialization, scaling-up is required to expand the fermentation process that is originally conducted on a laboratory scale to an industrial level. Z.-Z. Wang et al. BioDesign Research 7 (2025) 100002 2 Compared to DOE strategy, One-factor-at-a-time (OFAT) experiments are appropriate in preliminary screening and determining the optimal level of relatively independent factors [36]. However, the interaction between different factors cannot be ignored. The primary advantage of DOE lies in its capacity to evaluate the interactions among various factors while reducing the number of experiments required. For instance (see

### Table 2), Plackett-Burman Design is suitable for preliminary screening of

various factors and identifying dominant ones, which has been widely used in exploring the effects of mineral salts [37,38]. Orthogonal Design and Uniform Design are used to assess the correlations among various factors with different levels [39,40]. Central Composite Design [38,41] and Box-Behnken Design [42] are specially used for establishing mathematical relationships between variables and outputs. In general, when it is not clear which factors have relatively large impacts on the fermentation results and are most worthy of optimization, one can start with an Plackett-Burman Design [38], or a Minimum Run Resolution IV Design [41]. However, these methods cannot quantitatively assess the optimal level of each factor compared to other DOE strategies. Uniform Design requires relatively less workload, but sacriﬁces a certain degree of accuracy because it does not consider the equal occurrence of different level combinations. In case of three factors each of which are to be tested with three levels, Factorial Design, Central Composite Design, Orthogonal Design and Box-Behnken Design require 27, 15, 12 and 9 experimental tests, respectively (see Fig. 2). With a larger

### Fig. 2. Schematic diagram of DOE (Design of experiment) strategy. DOE is a comprehensive process development strategy, with capacity of evaluating the interactions

among various factors. Capital letters represent different factors. Greek numbers in (a)–(b) and pentagram dots in (c)–(f) represent test points. Test point is a single experiment combining different levels of factors. Z.-Z. Wang et al. BioDesign Research 7 (2025) 100002 3 number of test points, the workload will increase, and the evaluation of the impact of various factors and their interactions will be more accuracy. Therefore, DOE strategy should be reasonably selected according to the requirements of accuracy and experimental conditions. The selection of factor levels also signiﬁcantly inﬂuences the validity of the results; thus, it is essential to have a good understanding of the strain's characteristics and to consult relevant publications for guidance. What's more, it is important to note that when testing the inﬂuence of certain factors, it is necessary to keep other factors at an appropriate level based on experiences. Based on DOE, traditional statistical analysis methods such as ANOVA can be employed to preliminarily assess the impact of various factors and directly select the best level, but the construction of ML models can yield more precise predictive results. There is no strict correspondence between speciﬁc ML algorithm and particular experimental design strategy. Theoretically, different experimental design strategy may be suitable for different ML algorithms. However, multiple ML algorithms can be trained on the same set of data, and the best performing model can be selected afterwards [43]. Data obtained from Factorial Design, Orthogonal Design, Uniform Design, and Response Surface Methodology can all be utilized for ML model training. Furthermore, we can conduct a new batch of experiments based on previous experiment, and put them together for model construction. A typical example is constructing a new Central Composite Design experiment originating from Factorial Design [8].

## 3. Modelling methods for fermentation optimization

### 3.1. Overview

After obtaining fermentation data, ML algorithms can be employed for model building to predict the fermentation results under various conditions. This necessitates a certain level of proﬁciency in mathematical statistics and computer technology. In the age of AI, powerful tools have emerged, which drastically lower the barrier of usage and facilitate widespread application of ML across diverse research ﬁelds. Python, as one of the most popular programming languages for ML, has powerful packages such as Scikit-learn [44,45]. MATLAB also has a regression model toolbox containing various ML algorithms [46]. In addition to the algorithm, data quality also represents a critical factor that impacts the accuracy of the model. Thus, the fermentation data requires preprocessing before model training [47]. For example, each feature of data, such as sugar input (g/L) and temperature (C), has different value range, variance, and unit of measurement. By standardization or normalization, the preprocessed data preserves original characteristics, eliminates inconsistency and enhance model reliability. In the workﬂow of ML-based fermentation design and process optimization depicted in Fig. 1, variables involved in fermentation tests are limited and hypothetically inﬂuential, so the quality of data is only affected by experimental design and the accuracy of the fermentation result detection. However, if we acquire data from fermentor or even extract information from the literature, as presented in section 5 (Extension ideas of ML-based fermentation design), there may be some missing data or outliers in the dataset. Then, missing value handling and data cleaning should be applied to enhance data quality.

### 3.2. Response surface methodology

Response Surface Methodology (RSM) is not a ML method in the traditional sense, but it also attempts to ﬁnd the relationship between input variables and output variables. RSM is a simple and effective modelling method which can be completed by statistical analysis software [48]. Based on the steepness and shape of response surface, it is possible to observe the inﬂuence of each factor and the interactions. With regression equation, the optimal fermentation conditions can be predicted [38,41,42,49]. However, the model parameters constructed by RSM are relatively insufﬁcient. A second-order model only includes the ﬁrst and quadratic term (x, x2) of each factor, as well as the interaction term between two factors (x1x2), which lacks the ability to characterize the complex nonlinear interactions in fermentation system [48].

### 3.3. Typical ML algorithms

Decision Tree is a ML method based on tree structure [50]. This model can only predict the approximate range of fermentation result [51]. Random Forests (RF) is an integration algorithm of decision tree with improved accuracy [14]. By calculating the average of the predictions from all trees rather than taking the majority vote, Random Forests Regression (RFR) is capable of addressing regression tasks for fermentation optimization [52]. The advantages of RFR include rapid training speed, anti-interference capability and proﬁciency in handling high-dimensional data and capturing non-linear relationships. However, RFR is inevitably inﬂuenced by decision tree partitioning boundaries, and the predicted fermentation curve presents a discontinuous ladder shape, which is not suitable for describing the process of fermentation results changing over time [53]. Support Vector Machine (SVM) is a popular ML algorithm that uses hyperplanes to partition the sample space and achieve classiﬁcation of sample data [15]. The ﬁnal outputs in SVM are heavily inﬂuenced by a few key samples, indicating that the algorithm possesses not only simplicity but also robustness. In contrast, Support Vector Regression (SVR) is a regression algorithm speciﬁcally designed to identify hyperplanes that optimally ﬁt the data [54]. Experimental results indicate that SVR possesses robust ﬁtting and prediction capabilities especially for a small amount of data [55]. Therefore, if obtaining sufﬁcient fermentation data is difﬁcult due to limited experimental condition, SVR can be used for data ﬁtting and analysis.

### 3.4. Deep learning and other ML methods

Since 2012, deep learning based on neural networks has experienced a rapid expansion, with Backpropagation-Artiﬁcial Neural Network (BPTable 2 DOE strategy and examples. DOE strategy Example Product Productivity Reference Factorial Design Identifying mineral salts with signiﬁcant effect Ethanol 0.42 g/g sugar in 12 h from industrial wastewater [8] Plackett-Burman Design Studying the effects of supplementation of different mineral salts Ethanol 50.4 g/L after nutritional supplementation, 7.4-fold higher than non-supplemented [37] Orthogonal Design Optimization of temperature, fermentation time, initial pH and liquid loading volume (L9[34]) Amylase 20.59 U/ml under optimal conditions, 7-fold higher than initial condition [39] Uniform Design Optimization of glycerol, yeast powder, peptone, KH2PO4, MgSO4, and ZnSO4 (U10(106)) Ergosterol peroxide 256 μg/L, 5-fold higher than initial condition [40] Central Composite Design Identifying the effects of glucose, sodium glutamate and sea salt Docosahexaenoic acid (DHA) From 6.18 g/L to 8.33 g/L after optimization [38] Box-Behnken Design Optimization of initial pH, temperature, moisture content, and incubation period using alfa as sole carbon source Xylanase and CMCase 826.63 U/gds for xylanase activity, increased by 25 %. 61.17 U/gds for CMCase activity, increased by 26 % [42] Z.-Z. Wang et al. BioDesign Research 7 (2025) 100002 4 ANN) gaining popularity in the ﬁeld of fermentation optimization [53, 56–58]. BP-ANN simulates the synaptic structure of the brain with multiple neurons to process information [16]. Each neuron receives input signals from other neurons, combines them based on the weights assigned to each input neuron, and then produces an output signal through an activation function. In comparison to RSM, BP-ANN demonstrates superior prediction accuracy, and can theoretically capture almost any form of the nonlinear relationship. Due to its unique gradient descent training method and large number of parameters, BP-ANN has higher robustness than general ML algorithms. Apart from RF, SVM and BP-ANN, other ML methods have also been used in fermentation optimization (see examples in Table 3), like Gradient Boosting Machine (GBM) [59], Adaptive Boosting (AdaBoost) [59], eXtreme Gradient Boosting (XGBoost) [60,61], Gaussian Process Regression (GPR) [43], Bayesian Optimization [62], Functional Link Artiﬁcial Neural Network (FLANN) [63] and Least-Squares Support Vector Machine (LS-SVM) [64,65]. The development of new ML algorithms is always an ongoing process, but the No Free Lunch theorem states that there is no universal learning algorithm that performs well on all kinds of tasks [66]. In fact, some algorithms frequently perform better than others in certain cases. Thus, the understanding of the applicability of different ML algorithms is beneﬁcial for researchers.

## 4. Evaluation, hyperparameter tuning and optimal parameters

screening

### 4.1. Evaluation and hyperparameter tuning

Error is the discrepancy between model prediction and the actual result, which is utilized to assess the model's capability of ﬁtting and generalization. Mean Square Error (MSE), Root Mean Square Error (RMSE), and Correlation Index (R2) are commonly employed for model evaluation. Different algorithms possess distinct characteristics and perform well in different tasks, so researchers often train several models on the same set of data in order to identify the best algorithm [43]. ML models have hyperparameters that determines the model structure as well as the training procedure, which we must set to customize the model to speciﬁc data [67]. By carefully adjusting hyperparameters, we will help models to learn universal rules that can be applied to more potential samples. Tuning hyperparameters manually requires a high level of expertise from researchers, so automatic hyperparameter tuning methods, such as Grid Search, Random Search, Bayesian Algorithm and Genetic Algorithm have been developed. When dealing with models containing few hyperparameters and only running for a short time, complex algorithms are often unnecessary. Given that ML models used in fermentation optimization do not possess a certain level of complexity and can be ﬁnished within minutes, Huang chose to optimize hyperparameters for RFR, SVR, and BP-ANN models using Grid Search method to evaluate all combinations of hyperparameters [53].

### 4.2. Determination of optimum parameters

The main target of incorporating ML models into fermentation process is to identify the optimum parameters, or the combination of factors. By leveraging ML models to determine the best parameters, higher production efﬁciency can be achieved. While traditional fermentation optimization focuses on a single production indicator such as titer, we advocate for the necessity of Multi-Objective Optimization (MOO) to maximize economic beneﬁts. In MMO, a series of objectives are optimized simultaneously based on different levels of importance. For
